Inputs, buttons, and links look close to default browser controls with only 2–4px internal padding. Lists (plans, reviews) are tightly stacked with minimal space between items, prioritizing the number of visible records per viewport over spaciousness.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a simple vertical flow. The user first sees the bold program name at the top-left, then slightly to the right the global search bar on the same row. The eye then moves down to the breadcrumb line, and then to the large bordered content box. Inside the box, the eye travels from the top summary and plan information down to the save controls, then horizontally across the tab navigation links, then down into the active tab content. Within each tab, the flow is again top-to-bottom: for discussion, sort controls at top, then the review list, then the review form; for poll, the poll widget centered within its panel. Horizontal scanning within rows remains simple: left-to-right text and controls, no complex multi-column layouts.

layout.overall_description: The page looks like an old-school, text-heavy forum detail view laid out in a single narrow column on a white background. At the very top of this column, the program name appears as a bold, slightly larger text title aligned to the left, while a plain, functional global search form sits on the far right of the same line. Just below, a thin breadcrumb line stretches across the width, using simple text links separated by basic characters. Beneath this, a large rectangular box with a light border fills most of the visible page; this box is the main working area. Inside, content is stacked in tight vertical layers: a compact summary of program stats, a dense list of investment plans, a small row of save/bookmark links, and then a minimalist text-based tab strip. Immediately under the tabs, a single panel area shows either an overview (stats and plans), a discussion thread (sort controls, posts, and reply form), or a poll (simple radio buttons and a vote button). Margins and padding are modest, line spacing is tight, and everything feels utilitarian and efficient, evoking early 2000s forum layouts where text and data take precedence over visual decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not produce any visible UI. It conceptually sits at the very top of the page’s logic layer, initializing which tab is active and wiring up state transitions. Visually, it occupies no space, taking up neither width nor height, and does not affect the document flow. All other components render as if this controller were not present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no rendered box, no position in the layout. It only affects which of the tab panels is visible at a given time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is placed at the top-right of the main content column, sharing a single horizontal row with the program header. It consists of a simple text input and a small submit button, both using default browser styles with minimal 2–4px internal padding. Horizontally, it aligns flush to the right edge of the content column (about 16–24px inset from the browser window). Vertically, its baseline roughly aligns with the middle or bottom of the program header text on the left, forming a compact, utilitarian strip. There is only a small 8px vertical gap below this row before the breadcrumb line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow; no fixed or sticky behavior. It shrinks or expands horizontally with the page width but maintains right alignment within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb appears directly below the header+search row, spanning the full width of the content column. It consists of a single line of small text and links (e.g., “Home > Programs > [Program Name]”), left-aligned with the program header above. The breadcrumb line sits with a minimal top margin (~4–8px) beneath the header row and a small bottom margin (~8–12px) separating it from the main bordered content box. There is no background color; it appears directly on the white page background, emphasizing a thin, textual navigation cue between the title and the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block in the vertical flow; the text wraps if the viewport is narrow, but otherwise remains on a single line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program header is the primary page title and is placed at the top-left of the page, forming the left part of the top row that it shares with the global search bar. It is simple bold text (e.g., the program’s name), slightly larger than body text, with no background or decorative elements. It aligns to the left margin of the content column (about 16–24px from the browser edge). Horizontally, it occupies only the space required by the text, leaving flexible remaining space in the middle of the row before the global search bar on the right. Vertically, there is a modest top margin (~16px) from the top of the viewport and a small bottom margin (4–8px) before the breadcrumb. It clearly anchors the page but remains visually modest in line with classic forum titles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal flow; it does not stick or float and simply moves up or down with page scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program stats summary appears inside the main bordered content box at the very top of that box when the Overview tab is active. Within the box, it is the first block-level section, sitting just beneath the inner top padding (about 8–12px). It spans nearly the full width of the box, left-aligned, and is rendered as tightly spaced lines or a simple two-column text arrangement (e.g., key–value pairs such as 'Status: Paying', 'Min Deposit: $10'). There is minimal vertical space between individual lines (tight line-height) and a small 8px gap below the last stat row before the program plan list begins. There are no visual dividers beyond simple line breaks or basic table-like alignment if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the bordered box, always at the top of the Overview tab’s content area; it scrolls away with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program plan list sits directly below the program stats summary inside the main bordered content box, still within the Overview tab content. It occupies the full available width of the box, left-aligned, and is rendered as a vertical list of plans (either simple text lines or a basic table). The list is dense: each plan row uses minimal vertical padding (about 4px above and below), so multiple plans can fit in a short vertical span. The plan list sits about 8px below the stats summary and ends with a small 8–12px gap before the save controls row. There are no decorative backgrounds; only simple borders or underlines may appear if basic HTML tables are used.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the Overview tab; scrolls with the page and does not resize except to fit the width of the bordered box.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program save controls are positioned inside the main bordered content box below the plan list in the Overview tab, but functionally they should feel like a compact utility strip that applies to the whole program. They appear as a single horizontal line of small text links and/or basic form controls (e.g., 'Add to Watchlist', 'Bookmark', 'Blacklist'), left-aligned and taking only the height of a single text line. This strip sits about 8–12px beneath the plan list and roughly 8px above the tab navigation, acting as a thin divider between content data (stats/plans) and interaction/navigation (tabs). The controls have no special styling beyond default link appearance; spacing between individual actions is a few characters or a small 8px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/inline-block elements in a horizontal row within the bordered box; they wrap to a second line only on very narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation is placed immediately below the program save controls inside the bordered content box, spanning the full width of the box. It is a simple horizontal list of text links (e.g., 'Overview', 'Discussion', 'Poll') with the active tab shown in a minimal way—such as bold text, underlined, or slightly darker—but still in a plain text style. The tabs are left-aligned and sit on a single line under normal widths; small 8–12px gaps separate each tab label. A narrow 4–8px vertical gap below the tab labels separates them from the tab panel content area. There is no elaborate tab bar; the row looks like a simple inline list of links, consistent with early forum designs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row in the normal flow. It controls which tab panel is visible directly below, but the navigation itself does not move or stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview tab panel occupies the main content area inside the bordered box directly beneath the tab navigation. It spans the full internal width of the box and extends downward as needed. When active, it displays the program-stats-summary at its top, followed by the program-plan-list, and the program-save-controls can either appear just above the tabs or be visually perceived as part of the overview meta-section. Only one tab panel is visible at a time; when the Overview tab is selected, this panel is visible while the Discussion and Poll panels are hidden. Vertical spacing within the panel is minimal, with 8–12px gaps between its sections. The panel has no separate border; its edges are defined by the larger main box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that shares the same rectangular area with the other tab panels; its visibility is toggled by the tab navigation while its position remains fixed beneath the tabs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion tab panel shares the exact same spatial region as the overview panel: directly below the tab navigation and inside the bordered box. When the Discussion tab is active, this panel becomes visible and stretches from just under the tab labels down to the bottom of the box, expanding as needed. The discussion-sort-and-pagination controls appear at the top of this panel, followed by the discussion-review-list, and finally the discussion-review-form at the bottom. Content is stacked densely, leaving only small vertical gaps (8–12px) between these subsections. Only one of the tab panels is visible at a time, so while Discussion is active, Overview and Poll content are hidden but share the same footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that occupies the shared tab content area beneath the tab navigation; its visibility is toggled via the tab navigation without changing its layout position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the discussion tab panel, the sort and pagination controls form a narrow horizontal strip at the very top of the panel. Left-aligned options could include dropdowns or simple text links (e.g., 'Sort by date', 'Sort by rating'), while pagination links (e.g., 'Prev 1 2 3 Next') might align to the right of the same row or follow on the next line if space is limited. The controls sit just 4–8px below the tab navigation and about 8px above the first review item, acting as a compact control bar. The elements are dense, similar to classic forum pagination sections, with small gaps between individual links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline/inline-block controls inside the discussion panel; they remain at the top of the discussion area and scroll off-screen as the user scrolls down the list of reviews.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion review list sits immediately below the sort-and-pagination strip within the discussion tab panel. It fills the panel’s width and extends vertically as a series of stacked review entries, each review appearing as a text block with a small 8px gap beneath it before the next review. Reviews are dense: minimal line-height, limited padding around each item, and only simple separators (like a thin horizontal rule or an extra line break) between posts. The list can grow long and will typically occupy most of the vertical space in the discussion panel, pushing the review form further down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list that scrolls within the page; it expands as more reviews are loaded but remains confined to the width of the bordered box.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discussion review form is positioned at the bottom of the discussion tab panel, directly below the last review in the list. It spans most of the box’s width, left-aligned, and consists of basic form controls: a text area for the message, optional small inputs for subject or rating, and a single submit button. The controls are stacked vertically in a simple form layout, with 4–8px spacing between fields and no elaborate styling beyond the default browser UI. A small 8–12px gap separates the final review from the top of the form, and a similar gap exists below the submit button before the bottom padding of the bordered box. It feels like a natural continuation of the thread, in keeping with old forum reply areas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that always appears after the list of reviews in the discussion panel; as the list grows, the form is pushed further down and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The poll tab panel occupies the same tab content area as the overview and discussion panels, directly beneath the tab navigation inside the bordered box. When the Poll tab is active, this panel becomes visible and stretches to fit the poll content. It generally contains only the poll-widget, which is centered within the panel’s vertical stack. The panel uses the full internal width but typically only takes a modest vertical height unless poll results are long. There is a small 8–12px vertical gap between the tab navigation and the top of the poll widget, and similar bottom padding before the lower border of the main box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that shares the tab content area with the other panels; visibility is toggled by the tab navigation, and it scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the poll tab panel, the poll widget appears as a single compact block, left-aligned and spanning roughly the full width of the panel (minus the box padding). It consists of a simple poll question at the top, followed by vertically stacked radio buttons or checkboxes with their labels, and a single submit/vote button beneath them. If results are shown, they appear as tightly stacked text lines or basic bars below the options. The widget rests about 8–12px below the top of the poll panel and leaves a small 8–12px gap at its bottom. Spacing between options is minimal (about 4–8px), making the poll feel concise and in line with the utilitarian style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element inside the poll panel; it does not float or fix and simply grows vertically as more options or results are shown.

name: Saved & Blacklisted Programs
title: Saved & Blacklisted Programs
description: View and manage all programs you have saved as watchlist, bookmarks, or blacklist. Quickly jump to program details, change how each program is classified, or remove saved entries.

core_features: Load all saved programs grouped into watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist using getSavedPrograms, optionally filtered by save_type based on the filter_save_type URL parameter.
Provide tab-based navigation between views: all, watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist, with the active tab determined by the tab URL parameter.
Display saved programs with key Program information (such as status, risk level, ROI metrics, and running days) and SavedProgram metadata (such as notes and created_at).
Allow reclassification of a saved program between watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist using saveProgram, updating the save_type (and optionally notes) for the corresponding program.
Allow removal of a saved program from all lists using removeSavedProgram, regardless of its current save_type.
Support inline editing of personal notes on a saved program using saveProgram to update the SavedProgram.notes field.
Provide navigation from each saved program name to its detail page (program_detail.html?id=PROGRAM_ID).
Provide navigation back to the dashboard (dashboard.html) via a dedicated action.
Provide navigation to notification and risk alert settings (settings.html?section=notifications) via a dedicated action.
Include a global search form that uses searchSite to search across programs and forum threads, then navigates to a dedicated search results experience using URL parameters for the query.
user_workflows:
View all saved programs:
1. User opens saved_programs.html, optionally with a tab parameter.
2. On page init, the controller component reads tab and filter_save_type from the URL.
3. The controller calls getSavedPrograms with filter_save_type if present.
4. The response provides grouped watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist arrays.
5. The active tab is set according to the tab parameter (or defaults to 'all').
6. The list component renders saved programs for the active tab: all (combining groups) or a single group.

Switch between watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist:
1. User clicks a tab label (e.g., Watchlist, Bookmarks, Blacklist, or All).
2. The tab component updates the tab URL parameter to the selected value.
3. For tabs that correspond to a single save_type, the component also updates the filter_save_type URL parameter to that save_type; for the 'all' tab it removes filter_save_type from the URL.
4. The page controller detects the URL change and calls getSavedPrograms again with the updated filter_save_type (if any).
5. The list component re-renders with saved programs for the newly selected view.

Reclassify a program (e.g., from watchlist to blacklist):
1. In the saved program row, the user opens the classification control (e.g., a select box or menu).
2. The user chooses a new classification (watchlist, bookmark, or blacklist).
3. The row actions component calls saveProgram with programId for the associated Program.id, the chosen save_type, and the current notes (if any) so notes are preserved.
4. On a successful response, the controller updates the local representation of the SavedProgram entry with the returned saved_program.
5. If the program’s new save_type no longer belongs to the current tab (e.g., moving from watchlist to blacklist while viewing only the watchlist tab), the row is removed from the current list and appears in the appropriate group/tab.
6. Any relevant counts or summary indicators are updated as part of the state refresh.

Remove a program from saved items:
1. In the saved program row, the user clicks a remove action.
2. The row actions component calls removeSavedProgram with the associated Program.id.
3. On a successful response, the controller removes the corresponding SavedProgram entry from the in-memory lists for all groups.
4. The list component re-renders, no longer showing that program in any tab.
5. Counts and summaries update automatically based on the updated lists.

Edit personal notes for a saved program:
1. User opens the notes editor for a saved program (e.g., focuses a text field or clicks an edit control).
2. The notes editor displays the current SavedProgram.notes value.
3. User modifies the note text and triggers save (e.g., blur event, save button, or explicit submit).
4. The notes editor component calls saveProgram with programId, the existing save_type, and the updated notes string.
5. On successful response, the controller merges the returned saved_program into the current state so the updated notes are reflected in all views where this program appears.
6. If the request fails, the component can keep the previous notes in its state and show an error message, without hardcoding any default text.

Open program details from a saved program:
1. User clicks the program name in any tab.
2. The click handler reads the Program.id for that row.
3. The browser navigates to program_detail.html with the id URL parameter set to that Program.id (program_detail.html?id=PROGRAM_ID).
4. The user can then review full program information and return via browser back or site navigation.

Go to dashboard from the Saved Programs page:
1. User clicks a 'Go to Dashboard' action in the header or toolbar.
2. The click handler performs a navigation to dashboard.html without additional parameters.
3. The dashboard page loads according to its own logic.

Manage risk alerts and notifications:
1. User clicks a 'Manage Risk Alerts' action associated with notification settings.
2. The click handler navigates to settings.html with section URL parameter set to notifications (settings.html?section=notifications).
3. The settings page allows the user to adjust AccountSettings-related notification options; after changes, the user can return to saved_programs.html via navigation.

Use the global search from this page:
1. User types a keyword into the global search input.
2. On submit, the search form component reads the query text and optional type_filter (if provided in the UI for narrowing to programs or forums).
3. The component calls searchSite with query, type_filter, and paging parameters (page and page_size determined by the UI logic; no static values are assumed).
4. The page can either:
   - Display a transient list of results inline (program_results and forum_thread_results) with links to the relevant pages, or
   - Redirect the user to a dedicated search results page, passing the query and any type_filter as URL parameters.
5. When the user clicks a program result, the browser navigates to program_detail.html?id=PROGRAM_ID; when the user clicks a forum thread result, navigation targets the appropriate thread detail page defined elsewhere in the application.
interactions: On page load (or when URL parameters change), the controller parses tab and filter_save_type from the URL, calls getSavedPrograms with filter_save_type if present, and updates the lists bound to the tab and list components.
When the user clicks a tab, the tab component updates the tab URL parameter, adjusts filter_save_type according to the selected tab, and triggers a refresh via getSavedPrograms.
When the user clicks a saved program name, the router navigates to program_detail.html?id=PROGRAM_ID.

When the user changes a classification (e.g., selects 'blacklist' from a control on a saved row), the row actions component calls saveProgram with the chosen save_type and updates the row and relevant lists based on the response.

When the user clicks the remove action on a saved program row, the row actions component calls removeSavedProgram with programId and, on success, removes the corresponding entry from the rendered lists.
When the user edits notes and submits, the notes editor component calls saveProgram with the current save_type and new notes, then updates the SavedProgram.notes field in the displayed row.
When the user clicks 'Go to Dashboard', the navigation component simply redirects to dashboard.html.
When the user clicks 'Manage Risk Alerts', the navigation component redirects to settings.html?section=notifications.

When the user submits the global search form, the search component calls searchSite with the entered query, type_filter, page, and page_size, then either renders the results inline or redirects to a dedicated search results page with appropriate URL parameters.

When getSavedPrograms, saveProgram, removeSavedProgram, or searchSite return errors, the respective components can display non-hardcoded error messages derived from the returned message field (if provided) or from a generic error handler, without embedding static example data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters to control which subset of saved programs is shown and how data is fetched. The tab parameter determines the active view: 'all', 'watchlist', 'bookmarks', or 'blacklist'. If tab is missing or not one of the supported values, the page defaults logically to 'all' without storing this in localStorage. The filter_save_type parameter directly maps to the save_type used when calling getSavedPrograms. When filter_save_type is set to 'watchlist', 'bookmark', or 'blacklist', the controller passes that value as filter_save_type to getSavedPrograms, allowing the backend to limit results. When filter_save_type is absent, the controller calls getSavedPrograms without that filter, and all three arrays (watchlist, bookmarks, blacklist) are returned. The 'all' tab is implemented by leaving filter_save_type unset in the URL and in the call to getSavedPrograms, and showing all three groups combined in the UI. Switching tabs updates tab and filter_save_type in the URL (for single-category tabs) instead of using localStorage; this makes the state shareable via URL and ensures back/forward browser navigation restores the previous tab. Any client-side sorting, local text filtering, or expansion states (such as showing/hiding notes) are transient UI states and do not introduce additional persistent storage. The global search feature can append a query parameter (e.g., query) to a search results URL when navigating away, but this page’s persistent logic is strictly governed by tab and filter_save_type for saved program listing behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, reacts to URL changes for tab and filter_save_type, and orchestrates calls to getSavedPrograms, saveProgram, and removeSavedProgram. Holds the in-memory representation of watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist arrays and passes them to child components based on the active tab.

Provides tabs for 'All', 'Watchlist', 'Bookmarks', and 'Blacklist'. Reads the current tab from the URL and updates tab and filter_save_type URL parameters when the user selects a different tab, triggering the controller to refresh data as appropriate.

Displays aggregate indicators about the user’s saved programs, such as counts per save_type based on the arrays returned by getSavedPrograms. Provides a contextual 'Manage Risk Alerts' action that links to settings.html?section=notifications.

Provides high-level actions related to this page, such as navigating back to the dashboard and performing simple client-side filtering or sorting for the currently visible saved programs. All persistent classification and deletion actions are delegated to row-level components.

Renders the list of saved programs for the currently active tab. For the 'all' tab, combines watchlist, bookmarks, and blacklist groups into a single unified view while still exposing the save_type per row. For single-category tabs, displays only the relevant array. Delegates row-specific actions to the row component.

Represents a single saved program entry in the list. Shows key Program fields (such as name, status, risk_level, daily_roi_percent, min_deposit_usd, running_days, and other non-null values) and SavedProgram metadata (save_type, created_at, notes). Allows navigation to program details via the program name and exposes a dedicated area for actions and notes editing.

Provides per-row actions for reclassifying or removing a saved program. On reclassification, it calls saveProgram with programId from Program.id, the new save_type, and existing notes (if any). On removal, it calls removeSavedProgram with programId. It passes the results to the page controller to update the master lists.

Displays and edits the SavedProgram.notes field for a single saved entry. When the user saves changes, it calls saveProgram with programId, the current save_type, and the updated notes string. On success, it updates the notes shown in the row and notifies the controller.

Shows an appropriate message when there are no saved programs for the currently active tab based on the lists returned from getSavedPrograms. It does not use hardcoded example data; it simply checks list lengths from the controller state.

Provides a site-wide search input on this page. On submission, it calls searchSite with the entered query, optional type_filter, and paging parameters. It can either display a lightweight inline results panel or navigate to a dedicated search results page while passing query-related URL parameters.

Hosts navigation links relevant to this page: a link to the dashboard (dashboard.html) and a contextual link or button for risk alert settings (settings.html?section=notifications). It works in coordination with the summary bar and toolbar to centralize navigation behavior.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a simple, linear, top-to-bottom single-column flow. At the very top is a thin navigation links header. Below it, the page controller presents the page title and a short description as plain text. Immediately under that sits a compact, inline global search form (mirroring the mockup’s login strip pattern). Next comes the tab navigation in a single line (acting like a breadcrumb bar). Beneath that, a concise summary bar and then a slim toolbar line. The majority of the page is then occupied by a large bordered container holding the saved programs list, which either shows dense rows or, when empty, an inline empty state message.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a simple, linear, top-to-bottom single-column flow. At the very top is a thin navigation links header. Below it, the page controller presents the page title and a short description as plain text. Immediately under that sits a compact, inline global search form (mirroring the mockup’s login strip pattern). Next comes the tab navigation in a single line (acting like a breadcrumb bar). Beneath that, a concise summary bar and then a slim toolbar line. The majority of the page is then occupied by a large bordered container holding the saved programs list, which either shows dense rows or, when empty, an inline empty state message.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column runs nearly full-width, with modest left and right margins of about 16–24px from the browser edge. Vertical spacing between stacked sections (nav header, title block, search form, tabs, summary, toolbar, and list box) is tight, around 8–12px. The list container occupies almost all remaining vertical space, spanning roughly 90–100% of the content column’s width. Within the list, each row is a single compact line, with only a few pixels of padding above and below to maximize the number of rows visible per viewport.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is high: small line-height, minimal vertical padding, and no decorative spacing. Text sits close together, and controls (inputs, selects, buttons, links) have only 2–4px internal padding. Each line (tabs, summary, toolbar, and each program row) is treated as a tight strip of information, similar to old-school forum topic lists. This maximizes information per screen while remaining legible.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the very top-left with the navigation links row, then drops down to the bold page title and short description. From there it moves to the left-aligned search strip, then down to the tab navigation line, then through the summary and toolbar lines, and finally into the main vertical flow of the numbered program list. Within each row the eye travels left-to-right from the ordinal number, to the blue program name link, to the blue domain link, to the gray reply/count information and inline action links on the right. Scrolling continues straight down the list; no horizontal layout complexity interrupts this vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a very minimal, almost default-HTML-style investment forum tool: a white background with a left-aligned content column and modest side margins. At the top, a narrow strip of plain text navigation links leads into a bold, text-only page title and a single-sentence description. Directly underneath, a compact search form appears as a single line of label, input, and button. Beneath that, a one-line tab bar shows text links for Saved, Watchlist, and Blacklisted programs in a breadcrumb-like fashion. A short summary line follows, then a slim toolbar line with sorting and bulk action controls. The main focus is the large, light-gray bordered box filling most of the page, stretching nearly the full width of the content area. Inside is a dense ordered list: each program on one line with its number, blue links for program name and domain, a small gray reply/count note, and small textual action links aligned toward the right. When a row is expanded to edit notes, a simple textarea appears just below that line, lightly indented. The overall impression is compact, utilitarian, and text-forward, with a strong vertical scanning flow down the list and almost no decorative styling to distract from the data.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a single main content column running from the top of the viewport downward, aligned to the left with equal left and right margins of roughly 16–24px from the browser edge. Inside this column, it stacks all other sections vertically in order: navigation-links-header at the very top, then a small vertical gap (about 8px), followed by the page title in a bold or slightly larger font and the page description directly under it as a normal-weight line. This title-and-description block spans nearly the full width of the column but remains left-aligned, with no background color or box around it. Beneath the description, after another 8px gap, the global-search-form appears, then the tab-navigation, summary-bar, toolbar, and finally the large bordered list container. The controller does not introduce sidebars or multiple columns; it simply ensures all sections stretch fluidly with the window width within the fixed side margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the root flow container in normal document flow, scrolling with the page and not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits at the very top of the content column, directly under the browser’s top edge, forming a thin horizontal strip of text links (for example: Home, Forum, My Account). The links are inline and left-aligned, separated by simple characters like vertical bars or hyphens, with minimal horizontal spacing (4–8px) between them. There is no background color, no border, and no large padding—just a small vertical offset (about 4–8px) from the top edge of the content column. The strip extends horizontally as far as the links require but visually reads as a single line across the top-left portion of the page, leaving whitespace to the right. It occupies only a small fraction of the vertical space, less than 5% of the page height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow at the top; scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form appears as a compact inline row directly beneath the page title and description, separated by an 8–12px vertical gap. It is positioned at the left edge of the content column and spans only as much width as its controls need—roughly 30–40% of the column on a typical desktop. It contains a short label (e.g., “Search:”), a single-line text input, and a small submit button, all laid out horizontally on one line with 4–8px spacing between them. The input and button have minimal internal padding (2–4px) and no elaborate styling, resembling default browser controls on a white background. There is no enclosing border around the entire form. The form sits immediately above the tab-navigation, reinforcing a top-down flow similar to a login strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the natural document flow, not fixed; as the user scrolls, it moves out of view above the main list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation is placed directly below the global search form, with a small vertical gap of about 8px. It is a single horizontal line of text-based tabs (e.g., “All”, “Saved”, “Watchlist”, “Blacklisted”) aligned to the left edge of the content column. Each tab is a plain text link styled like a standard HTML link, possibly with the active tab rendered in bold or non-linked text. Tabs are separated by simple delimiters like vertical bars or angled brackets (e.g., Saved > Blacklisted), giving it a breadcrumb-like appearance. The entire bar occupies only the height of a single text line plus minimal line spacing and stretches horizontally only as far as the tabs need, leaving open white space to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In normal flow, stacked between the search form above and the summary bar below; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary bar appears directly below the tab navigation, with around 8px of vertical spacing. It is a single horizontal text row that begins at the left edge of the content column, containing concise counts such as “Saved: 12  |  Watchlist: 5  |  Blacklisted: 3”. The text is in the same font size as normal body text, with possibly the labels or numbers in a slightly bolder weight for emphasis, but still visually understated. There is no background fill or enclosing box; it floats on the white page. It spans only as wide as its text content requires, occupying roughly 30–50% of the row’s width on a typical screen, and leaves empty space to the right. The vertical height is only one line, continuing the tight, information-dense stacked layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the standard vertical stack; remains static and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits immediately below the summary bar, again separated by a small vertical gap of about 8px. It is a narrow, single-line strip of functional controls such as a sort dropdown (e.g., “Sort by: [Name ▼]”) on the left and optional bulk action controls (e.g., a select input and an “Apply” button) following to the right on the same line. All controls are basic HTML inputs and selects with minimal 2–4px padding and default styling. The entire toolbar is left-aligned and extends horizontally only as far as the controls require, typically covering 40–60% of the content column width, with empty space on the right. The row has no background color and no border; it reads visually as one more tight text-and-control line just above the main bordered list box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flowed directly above the main list container; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved programs list is the dominant visual element, located directly under the toolbar with a slightly larger vertical gap of about 12px to emphasize the transition into the main content area. It appears as a large, rectangular box that spans nearly the full width of the content column (around 90–100% of the available width, leaving just the column’s side margins) and extends vertically to fit as many rows as needed. The box has a simple 1px light gray border on all sides and a white background matching the page, with 8–12px of internal padding around its contents. Inside this container, programs are presented as a single ordered list: the numbers (1., 2., 3., etc.) aligned on the left margin followed by each program row on a separate line. Each row is tightly spaced with small line-height and only a few pixels of vertical gap between items, allowing many entries to appear in the viewport. When populated, the list fills the majority of the vertical screen space; when short, it still reads as the main content block due to its width and border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in normal document flow; as content grows, the box grows vertically and relies on page scrolling rather than internal scrollbars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each saved program row is a single, dense line within the ordered list inside the bordered container. The row begins with the ordinal number (e.g., “12.”) flush with the left edge of the list’s content area. Immediately after the number and a small space, the program name appears as a blue, underlined text link. A short hyphen or dash follows, then the program’s domain, also as a blue link. To the right of this core information, separated by a small gap, sits a smaller gray parenthetical note indicating reply or interaction counts (for example, “(18 replies)” or “(3 notes)”). On the far right of the same line, inline with the text, a short sequence of action links (e.g., “Details | Reclassify | Remove”) may appear, using the same font size but slightly separated from the main text by a cluster of spaces or a tab-like gap. All of this content sits on one line per row on larger screens, with no special background; rows are separated only by line spacing and the list’s natural line-height, typically showing 20–30 rows within a full viewport depending on screen size.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully inline within the ordered list; it expands to multiple lines only when the text wraps on very narrow screens, pushing subsequent rows downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The row actions are embedded at the right side of each saved program row on the same baseline as the program name and domain. They appear as a cluster of plain text links such as “Details”, “Move”, “Blacklist”, or “Remove”, separated by simple delimiters like vertical bars or spaces (e.g., “Details | Edit notes | Remove”). There is no special button styling—just standard blue underlined links with minimal spacing (4–8px) between them. On wider screens, they visually float toward the right half of the row because there is ample whitespace between the main program information and the actions; on narrower screens, they simply follow inline immediately after the gray reply note. They do not occupy a separate column or cell; instead, they share the same continuous text line, preserving the old forum-style look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline content with no fixed alignment; flows naturally with the text and wraps onto the next line on small screens as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When activated for a given program, the notes editor appears directly below that program’s row within the same bordered list container, pushing subsequent rows down. It is visually simple: a small label (e.g., “Notes:”) aligned with a slight indent from the left so that it starts under the program text rather than under the ordinal number, followed by a multi-line textarea that spans most of the container’s width (roughly 80–90% of the inner width). The textarea has basic browser styling with only 2–4px of internal padding and no heavy border beyond the default. Below or to the right of the textarea, on the same indented alignment, appear small inline buttons such as “Save” and “Cancel” styled as default HTML buttons. The vertical spacing above and below the notes editor is modest—around 8px—to keep the layout compact while clearly separating it from the adjacent rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands inline within the list; appears and disappears between rows without overlaying other content, increasing the height of the list container when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no saved programs to display, the empty state occupies the interior of the same bordered list container in place of any rows. Inside the box, the message is centered horizontally but aligned near the top vertically to maintain the dense, text-first look (rather than perfectly vertical centering). For example, a line such as “You have no saved or blacklisted programs yet.” appears as plain body text, possibly with a short suggestion on the next line. The empty state text is left-aligned with the container’s inner padding, not centered as a block, reinforcing the utilitarian feel. The border and 8–12px internal padding of the container remain unchanged, so the component still occupies a visually significant portion of the page even when empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the list rows: it appears only when the list has no items and remains within the same bordered box in normal document flow.

name: Dashboard
title: Dashboard
description: Personal dashboard showing the user’s saved HYIP programs that match their ROI and status preferences, along with recent risk alerts and quick actions.

core_features: Load and display a list of saved HYIP programs (watchlist, bookmarks, blacklist) that match the user’s dashboard-related AccountSettings using getDashboardOverview.
Surface recent RiskAlert items relevant to the user’s saved programs, with indicators on affected program cards using getDashboardOverview.
Allow changing a program’s saved type (watchlist, bookmark, blacklist) or removing it entirely via saveProgram and removeSavedProgram from within the dashboard.
Provide quick navigation from dashboard program cards to the corresponding program_detail.html page using the program id from getDashboardOverview.
Provide direct navigation to dashboard-related settings (settings.html?section=dashboard) and to the full saved programs management page (saved_programs.html).
Offer a global search input on the dashboard that queries programs and forum threads using searchSite and shows inline results without leaving the page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View my personalized dashboard
1. User navigates to dashboard.html (optionally with URL parameters like tab or view).
2. On page load, the dashboard-init-loader component calls getDashboardOverview with no parameters.
3. The response provides settings_snapshot, active_saved_programs, and recent_risk_alerts.
4. The dashboard-program-list component renders the subset of active_saved_programs that match any URL-based view filters (e.g., by save_type) while respecting the server-side filters already applied by getDashboardOverview.
5. The dashboard-risk-alert-list component renders recent_risk_alerts, optionally filtered by URL parameters (e.g., showing only unread alerts).
6. User can switch between dashboard tabs (e.g., programs vs alerts), which updates the URL parameters and re-renders visible components without re-calling getDashboardOverview unless the page is refreshed.

Workflow: Open program details from dashboard
1. User scans the dashboard-program-list to find a specific program.
2. User clicks anywhere on a program card’s main clickable area (excluding control buttons).
3. The dashboard-program-card component reads program.id from the bound Program object.
4. The browser navigates to program_detail.html?id={program.id} using a standard location change.

Workflow: Change how a program is saved (watchlist/bookmark/blacklist)
1. Within a program card, the user opens a save-type control (e.g., a menu or toggle).
2. User selects a new save_type value (watchlist, bookmark, or blacklist).
3. The dashboard-program-card component calls saveProgram with parameters: programId = program.id, save_type = selected value, notes = current note value (if any).
4. On success, the component updates the SavedProgram binding with the returned saved_program and message, and re-applies any view filters (for example, if the view URL parameter excludes the new save_type, the card disappears from the list).
5. On failure, an error message is displayed in the card without changing the current UI state.

Workflow: Remove a program from the dashboard saved list
1. User clicks a remove/un-save control on a program card.
2. The dashboard-program-card component calls removeSavedProgram with programId = program.id.
3. On success, the card is removed from the active_saved_programs list in the UI.
4. If the removal causes the current list to become empty, the dashboard-empty-state component becomes visible to suggest next actions (e.g., go to saved_programs.html or dashboard settings).

Workflow: Review and act on risk alerts
1. User switches to an alerts-focused view via a tab control or clicks an alerts-related link on the dashboard toolbar; this sets or updates the URL parameter tab=alerts.
2. The dashboard-risk-alert-list component reads recent_risk_alerts from getDashboardOverview and filters them based on the alerts_filter URL parameter (e.g., unread vs all).
3. User clicks a risk alert entry.
4. If the related program is present in active_saved_programs, the page scrolls to and visually emphasizes that program card; if not present, the user can still navigate to program_detail.html?id={program_id} from within the alert item.
5. The alert can be marked as read in the UI; the read/unread state is visual-only on this page unless managed by the backend via a separate interface (not defined here, so no state is persisted by this page).

Workflow: Use global search from dashboard
1. User types a query into the dashboard-search-form input.
2. On submit, the component calls searchSite with query = input value, type_filter = (if the user selected a scope), page = 1, and page_size = an application-configured value.
3. The component displays inline lists of program_results and forum_thread_results under the search box.
4. User clicks a program result to navigate directly to program_detail.html?id={program.id} or clicks a forum result to open the corresponding thread page (navigation target defined elsewhere in the application).
5. The query is reflected in the dashboard URL as q={query} so that reloading the page can re-trigger the same search automatically.
interactions: Page load: On window load or when the user navigates to dashboard.html, dashboard-init-loader triggers getDashboardOverview; while awaiting the response, loading indicators are shown in components that depend on the data.
Program card click: Clicking the main body of a program card (excluding controls) navigates the browser to program_detail.html?id={program.id}.
Save type change: Changing the save_type selection in a program card triggers a call to saveProgram; the UI is optimistically or pessimistically updated depending on success/failure responses.
Remove saved program: Clicking a remove/un-save button on a program card calls removeSavedProgram; on success, the card is removed from the displayed list.
Customize Dashboard: Clicking the 'Customize Dashboard' link in the toolbar navigates to settings.html?section=dashboard using a direct URL change.
Manage Saved Programs: Clicking the 'Manage Saved Programs' link in the toolbar navigates to saved_programs.html using a direct URL change.

Tab selection: Clicking a tab (e.g., Programs or Alerts) updates the tab URL parameter (tab=programs or tab=alerts) using the History API or a full reload; visible components update based on the new tab value.

View filter selection: Changing a view filter (e.g., to only show watchlist or only bookmarks) updates the view URL parameter (view=watchlist|bookmark|blacklist|all), and dashboard-program-list re-filters active_saved_programs on the client side using saved_program.save_type.
Alerts filter change: Selecting an alerts filter (e.g., unread vs all) updates the alerts_filter URL parameter, and dashboard-risk-alert-list filters recent_risk_alerts accordingly.

Risk alert click: Clicking an alert item either scrolls to the associated program card (if it exists in active_saved_programs) or navigates to program_detail.html?id={program_id} if the card is not visible in the current view.
Search input change: Typing in the search box updates the local query state; optionally, debounced calls to searchSite can be triggered for live suggestions if desired by the implementation.
Search submit: Submitting the search form calls searchSite; the inline search results area is updated with program_results and forum_thread_results; the query is also written to the q URL parameter.

Search result click: Clicking a program result from inline search navigates to program_detail.html?id={program.id}; clicking a forum thread result navigates to the thread’s URL (as defined in the broader application).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The dashboard page does not require any incoming URL parameters but supports optional parameters to control view state and behavior, following the rule that state is reflected in the URL rather than in localStorage.

Supported URL parameters:
- tab: Controls the major section of the dashboard.
  - tab=programs: The default view if tab is missing or unrecognized; dashboard-program-list is primary and dashboard-risk-alert-list is secondary or collapsed.
  - tab=alerts: Emphasizes dashboard-risk-alert-list; program cards may be minimized or shown in a secondary area.
  The dashboard-init-loader reads tab on load and passes it as a prop or configuration to child components; tab changes result in a URL update without changing server calls.

- view: Controls which saved program types are visible within active_saved_programs.
  - view=all: Show all saved types (watchlist, bookmark, blacklist); used when view is missing or unrecognized.
  - view=watchlist: Only show items where saved_program.save_type === 'watchlist'.
  - view=bookmark: Only show items where saved_program.save_type === 'bookmark'.
  - view=blacklist: Only show items where saved_program.save_type === 'blacklist'.
  This filtering is purely client-side based on the getDashboardOverview response; the server already ensures that only programs matching AccountSettings are returned.

- alerts_filter: Controls which recent_risk_alerts are shown in the dashboard-risk-alert-list.
  - alerts_filter=all: Show all alerts returned in recent_risk_alerts.
  - alerts_filter=unread: Show only alerts where is_read === false.
  If alerts_filter is missing, the component uses a default mode defined by the implementation, for example, showing all alerts.

- q: Represents the current search query in the dashboard-search-form.
  - If q is present on page load, dashboard-search-form initializes its input with q and may immediately call searchSite with page=1 and a page_size defined in application configuration.
  - When the user submits a new query via the search form, the component updates q in the URL to match the latest query.

Navigation and state updates:
- All UI controls that switch tabs, change view filters, or change alerts filters must update the corresponding URL parameters (using pushState/replaceState or full navigation) so that the current state can be bookmarked or shared.
- getDashboardOverview is always called without URL parameters; it uses server-side logic and the user’s AccountSettings to determine which programs and alerts to return. Client-side URL parameters only affect how that returned data is presented.
- The outgoing navigations defined for this page add or modify URL parameters on the target pages only (e.g., program_detail.html?id={program.id}, settings.html?section=dashboard) and do not introduce additional local client state on the dashboard itself.

components.functionality: Initializes the dashboard by reading URL parameters for tab, view, alerts_filter, and q, then calling getDashboardOverview to retrieve settings_snapshot, active_saved_programs, and recent_risk_alerts. It distributes this data and derived state (e.g., active tab, current filters) to child components and exposes a refresh mechanism.

Displays the page title, a summary derived from settings_snapshot (such as ROI and status preferences), and provides navigation actions to customize dashboard settings and manage all saved programs. It also exposes controls for switching tabs (programs/alerts) and selecting the program view (all/watchlist/bookmark/blacklist) and alerts_filter.

Provides a search input for querying programs and forum threads directly from the dashboard. On submit, it calls searchSite and renders inline search results; it also syncs the query with the q URL parameter. It can optionally perform debounced searches for live suggestions.

Renders a collection of program cards based on active_saved_programs provided by getDashboardOverview and filtered according to the view URL parameter and current tab. It passes per-item data to dashboard-program-card components, including whether each program has new alerts.

Represents a single saved program entry showing key metrics such as daily_roi_display, min_deposit_display, status_label, and risk-related indicators (has_new_alerts). It provides controls to change the saved type using saveProgram, remove the program using removeSavedProgram, and navigate to the program_detail.html page.

Displays recent_risk_alerts from getDashboardOverview, filtered by the alerts_filter URL parameter and current tab. Each alert can be clicked to focus or open the related program. Visual read/unread state can be derived from is_read, though any persistence of that state must be handled by the backend through other mechanisms.

Appears when active_saved_programs is empty after applying both server-side filters and client-side view filters. Provides functional links or buttons to navigate to settings.html?section=dashboard or saved_programs.html so the user can adjust settings or manage saved programs.

Optionally manages pagination or ordering for the program list on the dashboard if the number of active_saved_programs requires it. Pagination state can be reflected in additional URL parameters (e.g., page index) if implemented. It only operates on the data returned from getDashboardOverview and does not call any additional interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, left-aligned column of stacked sections that flow straight from top to bottom: page title (outside this spec), then the dashboard-toolbar as a thin horizontal strip, then the dashboard-search-form on its own line, followed by the dashboard-risk-alert-list as a compact bordered box, and finally the large dashboard-program-list box occupying most of the vertical space with dashboard-pagination-and-controls directly beneath it. Loader and empty-state content appear in place of the list area rather than as separate layout regions.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, left-aligned column of stacked sections that flow straight from top to bottom: page title (outside this spec), then the dashboard-toolbar as a thin horizontal strip, then the dashboard-search-form on its own line, followed by the dashboard-risk-alert-list as a compact bordered box, and finally the large dashboard-program-list box occupying most of the vertical space with dashboard-pagination-and-controls directly beneath it. Loader and empty-state content appear in place of the list area rather than as separate layout regions.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width and is offset from the left and right edges by about 16–24px. Vertically, each section is separated by small 8–12px gaps to preserve the compact, utilitarian feel. The dashboard-program-list is the dominant block, taking up around 60–70% of the vertical space in a typical viewport, while the toolbar, search form, and alert list collectively use only the upper 20–30%. Pagination and controls sit in a narrow strip just under the list.
layout_strategies.content_density: All components follow a high-density, old-forum style: small font size, tight line-height, and minimal internal padding. Inline controls (inputs, buttons, links) have only 2–4px internal padding, and list rows in the program list and alert list have about 4–6px vertical padding. There are no decorative gaps or oversized margins; whitespace is only what’s needed for legibility.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strictly vertical and left-to-right within each line. The user’s eye starts at the page title and immediately moves to the right along the dashboard-toolbar strip, then drops down to the left-aligned dashboard-search-form, then to the dashboard-risk-alert-list box, and finally into the dashboard-program-list, scanning each row from left (ordinal/primary text) to right (status, counts, controls). Pagination at the bottom provides a natural stopping point before scrolling.

layout.overall_description: The dashboard reads like an old-school forum topic index: a plain white background with a single, left-aligned column of text-based sections. Near the top, a slim toolbar line sits just under the page title, followed by a compact, inline search form that feels like a simple browser-default form. Beneath that, a small bordered box lists recent risk alerts in tight lines of text. The eye then falls into a larger bordered box that fills most of the page height, containing a dense list of saved HYIP programs—each program on its own single line with a number, blue text links, and small gray metadata, echoing forum thread lists. At the very bottom, a narrow strip of pagination links and basic list controls anchors the layout. Everything is text-first, with thin borders, small vertical gaps, and no decoration, creating a utilitarian, information-heavy dashboard that mirrors early 2000s forum pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears in the exact vertical slot where the main data content would normally sit, preserving the single-column flow. It is placed below the dashboard-toolbar and dashboard-search-form (and risk alerts, if those load separately) and above the pagination strip. Visually, it occupies roughly the same width as the dashboard-program-list—about 80–90% of the viewport, left-aligned with 16–24px side margins. When active, the loader either shows as a single line of status text (e.g., 'Loading…') centered horizontally within a light, thin rectangular border, or as plain text aligned to the left inside that area, in keeping with the bare-bones style. The height is modest (about 48–80px), enough to make the loading state noticeable but not dominant; it never overlays the header or toolbar, and it does not dim the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, it replaces the dashboard-program-list (and optionally the risk-alert list) while data is loading. Once content is available, the loader is removed and the list/alerts occupy its space; it does not float or stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar is a thin, full-width strip positioned at the top of the main column, immediately below the page title and above the search form. It spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, aligned to the left with 16–24px horizontal margins from the browser edge. Vertically, it is compact—about the height of a single line of text plus 4–6px padding above and below. Inside, all controls (e.g., 'Add Program', 'Refresh', or view toggles) are laid out in a single horizontal row with minimal spacing (4–8px) between them, mimicking the inline login strip from the mockup. Text labels and links are left-aligned within this strip; if there are right-aligned actions, they are separated by a wide gap of flexible whitespace, not by additional visual elements. There is no background color beyond white; at most, a thin bottom border can subtly separate it from the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the top of the content column. It scrolls away with the page content and does not remain fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits directly beneath the toolbar, separated by a small 8–12px vertical gap. It reads as a single-line, inline form aligned to the left edge of the main column, taking up only as much width as its fields require, with empty whitespace extending to the right. A typical arrangement is: a plain text label ('Search:'), followed immediately to the right by a text input, then small inline controls like a status dropdown or ROI filter, and finally a simple submit button. Each control has minimal internal padding (2–4px) and small 4–8px gaps between them. The form height matches a standard browser text input line, about 28–32px total, so it feels compact like a login strip. There is no enclosing box; at most, inputs display their native borders, and the search form relies on its position and tight spacing to be recognizable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow, directly under the toolbar and above the risk-alert list. It expands horizontally only as needed by its contents and collapses naturally on narrower screens without introducing additional rows unless the viewport is extremely narrow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The risk alert list is a compact bordered box placed beneath the search form, separated from it by a modest 8–12px vertical gap. It is left-aligned with the main column and spans 80–90% of the viewport width with consistent 16–24px side margins, matching the width of the main program list below. The box is outlined with a simple, thin gray border and has light internal padding of about 8–12px on all sides. Inside, alerts are presented as a dense vertical list (similar to forum posts): each alert occupies a single line or at most two lines of text, with about 4–6px vertical padding between items. Alert text is left-aligned, using basic bold or simple ALL CAPS prefixes (e.g., 'ALERT:') rather than icons or color blocks. There is no header bar; if a title like 'Recent Risk Alerts' is present, it appears as the first line inside the box in bold, with the actual alerts listed directly underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly between the search form and the program list. If there are no alerts, the component either collapses entirely (no box rendered) or shows a single narrow line of subdued text within the same border footprint, keeping the vertical rhythm consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program list is the dominant content block, placed directly beneath the risk-alert list with a small 8–12px vertical gap. It spans almost the full width of the main column—about 80–90% of the viewport—left-aligned and sharing the same 16–24px side margins as the alert box above. The list is enclosed in a rectangular box with a thin gray border and around 8–12px of internal padding. Inside, items are arranged as a dense vertical list reminiscent of old forum thread lists: each program occupies a single row with minimal vertical padding (4–6px above and below) and tight line-height. Rows are not card-like; there are no heavy backgrounds or shadows, just text with occasional thin horizontal dividers if needed. At the left of each row is a small ordinal number (1., 2., 3., etc.), followed by the program name as a blue, underlined link, then a hyphen, then the program domain as a blue link or plain text. To the right, inline metadata such as ROI, status, or last update appears in regular or gray text, ending with a smaller gray parenthetical note (e.g., '(3 alerts)' or '(saved 2 days ago)'). All content is kept on one line when possible; if wrapping is needed for long titles, wrapped text aligns under the main text, not under the number. The list height naturally extends downward and typically fills 60–70% of the viewport height on initial load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the main scrollable block. As the user scrolls, rows simply continue downward. It does not fix columns or headers; any header row (such as column labels) is implemented as the first list row inside the border, not as a floating element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program card is visually a single, compact text row inside the program list box rather than a distinct card-style panel. It spans nearly the full internal width of the dashboard-program-list container, with about 8–12px left padding from the list’s border and 4–6px vertical padding per item. On the far left, a small ordinal number is aligned in a narrow, implicit column. Immediately to its right, the program name appears as a standard blue, underlined HTML link, followed by a short hyphen and the program domain as another blue link or plain text. This sequence forms a strong left-to-right reading path. After a small gap (8–12px), additional details (ROI percentage, payment status, or plan type) appear as plain text. At the far right end of the row, in lighter gray and slightly smaller text, a parenthetical note summarizes a key metric (for instance, '(2 recent alerts)' or '(matches ROI filter)'). All of this sits on a single baselined text line when possible; only program names or domains wrap, and when wrapping occurs, the second line aligns with the program name rather than the ordinal number, preserving a clean left column of numbers. Rows may be separated by very thin, subtle horizontal rules or simply by their vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list; cards are stacked vertically in strict order with no masonry or multi-column behavior. They expand horizontally to fill the parent container and vertically only as needed to accommodate text wrapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The risk alert list is a compact bordered box placed beneath the search form, separated from it by a modest 8–12px vertical gap. It is left-aligned with the main column and spans 80–90% of the viewport width with consistent 16–24px side margins, matching the width of the main program list below. The box is outlined with a simple, thin gray border and has light internal padding of about 8–12px on all sides. Inside, alerts are presented as a dense vertical list (similar to forum posts): each alert occupies a single line or at most two lines of text, with about 4–6px vertical padding between items. Alert text is left-aligned, using basic bold or simple ALL CAPS prefixes (e.g., 'ALERT:') rather than icons or color blocks. There is no header bar; if a title like 'Recent Risk Alerts' is present, it appears as the first line inside the box in bold, with the actual alerts listed directly underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly between the search form and the program list. If there are no alerts, the component either collapses entirely (no box rendered) or shows a single narrow line of subdued text within the same border footprint, keeping the vertical rhythm consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state is rendered inside the same spatial footprint as the dashboard-program-list. When there are no saved programs or no matches for the current filters, the bordered list box still appears in its usual position below the risk-alert list, but instead of multiple rows it contains a single block of explanatory text. This text is left-aligned near the top-left of the box, with 12–16px of top padding inside the border and 8–12px side padding, maintaining the same overall box size and margins. The message uses regular body text, possibly with a short bolded lead-in ('No programs found.') followed by plain text guidance or a link to add programs, all on one or two lines. There are no illustrations, icons, or large centered banners; the empty state feels like a simple forum message row, preserving the page’s high-density, utilitarian character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with populated list rows. It occupies the list container whenever there is no data and disappears as soon as program rows are available, without altering the placement of surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination and list controls sit immediately below the dashboard-program-list box, separated by a small 8–12px vertical gap. This region is a thin horizontal strip spanning the same width as the list (80–90% of the viewport, left-aligned with 16–24px side margins). Content is arranged inline on a single line to preserve density: on the left, text like 'Pages:' followed by clickable page numbers and 'Next/Previous' links in plain blue link style; on the right, optional small controls such as a 'Per page' dropdown or a 'Sort by' select. The two sides are separated by flexible whitespace rather than visual dividers. The height is comparable to a single text line with 4–6px vertical padding, making this strip discrete and light. There is no surrounding box or background—only the links’ native appearance and any default browser borders on selects or inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content area, following the list. It scrolls with the content and does not stick; on very small screens its inline elements may naturally wrap to a second line but still maintain a simple top-to-bottom reading order.

name: Settings
title: Account Settings
description: Configure your default display currency, dashboard filters, and in-site notification preferences (including scam and risk alerts) for HYIP program listings and watchlists.

core_features: Load current account settings (currency, dashboard filters, notification preferences) using getAccountSettings when the page is opened or refreshed.
Provide tabbed navigation between settings sections (e.g., dashboard display, currency, notifications) controlled by the URL parameter section.
Allow users to adjust dashboard display filters (minimum daily ROI, inclusion of closed programs, status filters) and save them via updateAccountSettings.
Allow users to set a default display currency for ROI, deposits, and returns across the site, and persist it using updateAccountSettings.
Allow users to enable or disable in-site watchlist risk/status change notifications and save this preference via updateAccountSettings.

Allow users to subscribe or unsubscribe from in-site scam alerts for problematic programs using toggleScamAlertsSubscription, and keep this in sync with AccountSettings.notifications_scam_alerts_in_site.
Provide a direct link from the scam alerts settings area to the problematic programs view on programs.html with the appropriate URL parameter for reviewing the scam list.
Expose a global header search form that uses searchSite to validate or prefetch results before navigating to the dedicated search results page.
user_workflows: Change dashboard filters and apply them: (1) User navigates to settings.html (optionally with section=dashboard). (2) Page calls getAccountSettings and populates the dashboard settings form with current values. (3) User adjusts minimum daily ROI, chooses whether to show or hide closed programs, and selects which program statuses to include. (4) User clicks a Save or Apply button for the dashboard section. (5) Component calls updateAccountSettings with the modified AccountSettings fields. (6) On success, the page updates in-memory settings and shows feedback. (7) User clicks the View Dashboard control to navigate to dashboard.html, where the dashboard uses the updated settings to filter programs.

Set default display currency: (1) User opens settings.html (optionally with section=currency). (2) Page loads existing settings using getAccountSettings and sets the selected default_currency in the currency form. (3) User selects a new default currency from the supported enum values. (4) User submits the currency settings form. (5) Component calls updateAccountSettings with the new default_currency while preserving other settings. (6) On success, the component updates the visible selected currency and shows confirmation. (7) When the user views other pages such as dashboard.html or programs.html, ROI and deposit values are normalized to the new default currency according to those pages’ logic.

Configure watchlist risk/status notifications: (1) User navigates to settings.html?section=notifications or selects the Notifications tab. (2) Page retrieves existing notification preferences via getAccountSettings and sets the initial state of watchlist-related notification controls. (3) User toggles the control for in-site notifications on status or risk changes of watchlisted programs. (4) User submits the notification settings form. (5) Component calls updateAccountSettings with the updated notifications_watchlist_risk_in_site field. (6) On success, the page reflects the new toggle state and shows feedback. Future risk alerts generated for watchlisted programs are shown or suppressed in-site according to this preference.

Subscribe/unsubscribe from scam alerts and review problematic programs: (1) User is on the Notifications tab of settings.html. (2) Page displays the current scam alerts subscription state based on AccountSettings.notifications_scam_alerts_in_site. (3) User toggles the scam alerts subscription control. (4) Component calls toggleScamAlertsSubscription with subscribe set to the desired boolean state. (5) On success, component updates AccountSettings.notifications_scam_alerts_in_site in memory using the returned value and shows a confirmation message. (6) If the user wants to see which programs are currently flagged as problematic, they click a Review Scam List link or button in the same area, which navigates to programs.html?view=problematic_programs.

Switch between settings sections using URL parameter: (1) User opens settings.html without a section parameter, and the page chooses a default section (for example, dashboard-related settings) to display. (2) If the user clicks another tab, the tab navigation component updates the URL to settings.html?section={selected_section} using navigation or history APIs without reloading or with a reload as desired by implementation. (3) When section changes, the page conditionally renders the relevant settings form and keeps other sections hidden. (4) If the user returns to the settings page from another page using a link with a specific section parameter (e.g., settings.html?section=notifications), the page immediately opens with that section active.

Use global search from the settings page: (1) User types a keyword into the header search input while on settings.html. (2) On form submit, the search component calls searchSite with the query, an optional type_filter, and paging arguments defined by the component logic. (3) The component may optionally use the returned program_results and forum_thread_results for inline validation or suggestions. (4) After the API call resolves, the component navigates to the central search results page (for example, search.html) including the query (and any relevant filters) in the URL parameters, where search results are rendered using that page’s logic.
interactions: On initial page load or when the user refreshes settings.html, the page calls getAccountSettings once to retrieve the current AccountSettings and uses the response to populate all settings-related components.

When the user clicks on a tab in the settings tab navigation, the active section changes, the section URL parameter is set or updated, and the corresponding settings form becomes visible while others are hidden.

When the user modifies dashboard filter inputs (minimum ROI numeric input, closed programs visibility toggle, status multi-select) and submits, an updateAccountSettings call is made with those updated fields, and the response is used to confirm and re-bind form values.
