There is a small vertical gap (about 8px) above and below it, matching the compact stacking seen in the mockup. No extra container box or background is used—just inline links separated by simple characters (like '>' or '»'). It visually anchors the thread within the forum hierarchy before the eye drops into the main bordered content box that follows immediately below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width inline text line within the normal document flow; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread header appears at the very top-left of the main content area as a bold, larger-size text line displaying the thread title. It occupies roughly the left 60% of the available content width. On the same horizontal row, the global search form is aligned to the right, so the header text starts at the left margin and the search controls end near the right margin. Vertical padding around the header is minimal—about 8px above and 4–8px below—keeping it tight against the top edge of the content and the breadcrumb below. The header does not sit inside any bordered box; it is plain text on white, reinforcing the simple, utilitarian look. If additional thread metadata (e.g., author, created date) is present, it appears as a small, light-weight text line directly under the title, still left-aligned, using tight line spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element at the top of the main content column; shares its horizontal row with the right-aligned global search form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread actions bar is a thin horizontal strip located inside the main bordered thread box, attached to its top interior edge. It spans the full inner width of the box. Within this strip, actions such as sort order, 'Reply to Thread', and possibly 'Subscribe/Unsubscribe' links are presented as plain text links or simple select/dropdown controls. These controls are left-aligned and arranged inline on a single line with tight spacing between each (about 8px). The bar itself has no background color beyond the white of the page; its separation from the rest of the content is implied by its position immediately under the border and a small 4–8px gap below it before the first pagination strip. The vertical height of this bar is minimal, just enough for a single line of text and controls, maintaining a dense top-of-thread control cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the main bordered container; scrolls with the content and maintains the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post list occupies the central, largest portion of the main bordered thread box and is vertically stacked between the top pagination strip and the bottom pagination/reply area. It spans nearly the full inner width of the box, with about 8–12px padding on the left and right. Posts are rendered as a sequence of block items, one directly below the next, with very small vertical gaps (4–8px) or thin separating rules between them. Each post begins with a compact header row showing post number, author, and timestamp aligned near the left edge, using small text with tight line spacing. Below that, the message body flows left to right, wrapping on the same width as the container with no additional side columns. There is minimal padding around the message text—just enough (about 8px total vertically) to keep lines from touching the separators. The overall effect is a dense column of posts that fills most of the vertical height of the bordered box, encouraging continuous downward reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width vertical stack within the bordered container; grows in height with more posts and pushes subsequent elements downward in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are compact lines of small text links positioned as thin strips closely associated with the post list. One instance appears directly above the first post, just below the thread actions bar, and another instance appears directly below the last post and just above the reply form. Each strip is left-aligned, starting at the inner left padding of the main bordered box, with page numbers and 'Previous/Next' links placed inline and separated by small gaps (about 4–8px). The strips occupy only the height of a single text line plus minimal vertical margin (4–8px above and below), keeping them visually tight to the post list. There is no extra framing or visual emphasis beyond the blue links themselves, consistent with the minimal forum aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeated small horizontal blocks within the bordered container; each instance sits in the normal vertical flow—one above and one below the post list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reply form is positioned at the bottom of the main bordered thread box, directly beneath the lower pagination strip. It spans almost the full inner width of the container, aligned with the left and right padding used by the posts. The form elements are arranged in a simple, stacked layout: a small 'Quick Reply' label or heading, followed by a text area for the message that occupies most of the width, and then a single horizontal row of compact controls (e.g., 'Post Reply' button, optional 'Preview' button, and maybe a checkbox) aligned left. Inputs and buttons use minimal internal padding (2–4px), and vertical spacing between the textarea and buttons is tight (about 4–8px). The form’s top edge is separated from the pagination by only a narrow gap, preserving the high-density feel. No extra background shading is used—only the default browser styling and the surrounding border of the main container frame it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the bordered container; expands vertically if the textarea is resized but remains within the normal page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription status indicator appears as a small, light-colored text snippet positioned inside the main bordered thread box along the same horizontal band as the thread actions bar but aligned to the right edge. On that top strip, thread actions occupy the left side while the subscription status (e.g., 'Subscribed to this thread' or 'Not subscribed') appears on the far right, using muted gray text and a smaller font size to keep it visually subtle. It shares the same minimal vertical height as the actions bar, ensuring the entire control band is only one text line tall. The text is right-aligned with about 8–12px padding from the inner right border of the box. This placement allows the user to catch subscription info quickly in peripheral vision while keeping the main reading flow focused downward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element positioned within the top actions strip of the bordered container, right-aligned on the same line; scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is a compact, inline form positioned in the top row of the page, sharing the line with the thread header. It is right-aligned relative to the main content column, occupying roughly the right 40% of the row. The form elements—label (or placeholder), a single text input, and a small submit button—are laid out horizontally in one line, with only minimal internal padding in the input and button (2–4px). The distance between each element is small (4–8px), matching the tight style of the login strip in the mockup. Vertically, the search form aligns with the baseline of the thread title, creating a continuous horizontal visual line from the left-aligned title to the right-aligned search controls. There is only a narrow gap (around 8px) below this row before the breadcrumb line, preserving the compact, stacked top-of-page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block form on the first visible row; floated or flex-aligned to the right within the header row, remaining within the normal document flow.

name: New Forum Thread
title: New Forum Thread
description: Form for creating a new forum thread in a specific category (e.g., New HYIPs), capturing title, prefix, body, optional disclaimer, and publishing via the createForumThread interface.

core_features: Read the categoryId URL parameter to determine in which ForumCategory the new thread will be created.
Display a new thread creation form with fields for thread title, optional prefix (ForumThread.prefix enum), rich-text body, and an option to include a standard disclaimer line.
Use the createForumThread interface to submit the new thread (including categoryId, title, prefix, body, disclaimer_included).
On successful thread creation, redirect the user to forum_thread.html with threadId set to the created ForumThread.id.
On failed thread creation, display error feedback returned by createForumThread and allow the user to correct inputs and resubmit.
Provide a global search bar that uses the searchSite interface to redirect to a dedicated search results page with the current query.
Provide optional inline search suggestions for existing forum threads when the user types a title, using the searchSite interface to help avoid duplicate announcements.
Provide navigation controls (Cancel / Back to Category) that send the user back to forum_category.html with the existing categoryId URL parameter.
user_workflows: Create and publish a new thread: (1) User lands on forum_new_thread.html with categoryId in the URL. (2) Page validates that categoryId is present; if valid, it enables the form. (3) User enters a thread title. (4) User selects an optional prefix from the ForumThread.prefix enum options. (5) User enters the body content in the rich-text editor, including any program details they want. (6) User toggles whether to include a standard disclaimer line; UI reflects whether disclaimer_included will be true or false. (7) User clicks the Post Thread button, triggering createForumThread with categoryId, title, prefix, body, disclaimer_included. (8) If createForumThread.success is true, the page redirects to forum_thread.html?threadId={thread.id}. (9) If createForumThread.success is false, the page displays the returned message and keeps user input so they can adjust and resubmit.

Cancel thread creation and go back to category: (1) User visits forum_new_thread.html with categoryId in the URL. (2) At any time, the user clicks Cancel or Back to Category. (3) The page reads the current categoryId from the URL and navigates to forum_category.html?categoryId={categoryId}, discarding any unsaved input in the current view.

Use global search while on the New Thread page: (1) User focuses the global search bar and enters a query string. (2) On search form submission, searchSite is called with the query and chosen type_filter/page/page_size parameters. (3) The page navigates to the central search results page (outside this spec) with the query encoded as a URL parameter (for example search.html?query={query}), so that page can render the searchSite results. (4) User may return to forum_new_thread.html with the original categoryId preserved in navigation history.

Check for similar existing threads before posting: (1) User starts typing a thread title in the title field. (2) After a debounced delay or when the title loses focus, the page calls searchSite with query equal to the current title input and type_filter configured to search only forum threads. (3) When searchSite returns forum_thread_results, the page shows a small inline list of matching ForumThread titles as suggestions or warnings. (4) User can optionally open suggested threads in new tabs to verify if a similar HYIP program thread exists before finalizing their own thread.
interactions: Typing in the Title field updates the local form state and may trigger an inline search via searchSite for potentially duplicate forum threads.
Changing the Prefix dropdown sets the selected value from the ForumThread.prefix enum to be sent with createForumThread on submission.
Editing the Body rich-text area updates the body string that will be submitted to createForumThread; formatting controls affect only how the body string is generated.
Toggling the Disclaimer option (e.g., a checkbox or switch) sets disclaimer_included to true or false and updates any local preview to show or hide standard disclaimer text configured elsewhere.
Clicking Post Thread validates required inputs (e.g., title, body, presence of categoryId), then calls createForumThread and shows a loading state until the response is received.
If createForumThread responds with success=false, the page shows the message returned and keeps all field values intact so the user can modify and resubmit.
If createForumThread responds with success=true, the page immediately navigates to forum_thread.html?threadId={thread.id}.
Clicking Cancel or Back to Category reads the current categoryId from the URL and navigates to forum_category.html?categoryId={categoryId}, skipping any call to createForumThread.
Submitting the Global Search form calls searchSite with the current query and passes the query into the URL of the search results page to load full results there.
Clicking on any suggested thread in the duplicate-checker area opens that thread in forum_thread.html using its ForumThread.id in the threadId URL parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads the categoryId URL parameter on load. If categoryId is missing or invalid according to server-side validation, the thread creation form is disabled or an error state is shown, and Post Thread is prevented from calling createForumThread. The active categoryId is persisted in all navigations initiated from this page that return to the category (forum_category.html?categoryId={categoryId}). The new thread form state (title, prefix selection from ForumThread.prefix, body content, and disclaimer_included flag) is kept in memory for the lifetime of the page; it is not stored in localStorage. Any inline duplicate-check search uses the current title value but does not alter the URL. The global search bar can update the browser URL to a separate search results page with query and optional type_filter/page/page_size as URL parameters, but that navigation occurs away from forum_new_thread.html. After a successful createForumThread call, the page redirects to forum_thread.html and appends threadId={createdThread.id} to the URL based on the Thread returned by the interface.

components.functionality: Provides a site-wide search input that allows the user to search for HYIP programs and forum threads from the New Forum Thread page. On submission, it uses searchSite with the given query and chosen type_filter/page/page_size, then navigates the user to a dedicated search results page that will display the returned program_results and forum_thread_results.

Main form allowing the user to create a new ForumThread and its first ForumPost. It collects the thread title, prefix (ForumThread.prefix enum), rich-text body, and a boolean disclaimer_included. On submission, it calls createForumThread with categoryId from the URL and the form values, then handles success or error responses and navigation.

Listens to changes in the thread title field and, when the title is sufficiently long or loses focus, calls searchSite with query equal to the title and a type_filter that restricts results to forum threads. It then displays a small list or count of matching ForumThread results so the user can review similar topics before posting.

Provides a control (such as a checkbox or switch) that represents the disclaimer_included boolean. When enabled, it visually indicates that a standard disclaimer line will be appended or otherwise associated with the thread content upon creation; when disabled, no disclaimer will be included. The resulting disclaimer_included value is passed directly to createForumThread.

Shows a live preview of the new thread as it would appear once published, using the current title, selected prefix, body content, and disclaimer_included flag. It does not make API calls itself but reflects the local state that will be sent to createForumThread.

Manages the asynchronous state of the createForumThread call. It sets loading states while the request is in progress, processes the success boolean and message returned, triggers navigation to forum_thread.html?threadId={thread.id} on success, and surfaces any error messages on failure without losing the user’s input.

Renders navigation actions like Cancel or Back to Category. When activated, it reads categoryId from the current URL and navigates to forum_category.html?categoryId={categoryId}, allowing the user to exit the new thread creation flow.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict single-column, top-to-bottom flow. At the very top-left is the page title (outside the scope of these components), followed immediately by a horizontal utility strip containing the global search bar. Directly under that, a single-line breadcrumb-style category navigation row. Below the navigation, a large bordered main content box holds the thread creation form and its related helpers (duplicate checker, disclaimer toggle, submission status messaging). The optional thread preview appears as a separate bordered box stacked directly beneath the main form, all left-aligned with consistent side margins.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict single-column, top-to-bottom flow. At the very top-left is the page title (outside the scope of these components), followed immediately by a horizontal utility strip containing the global search bar. Directly under that, a single-line breadcrumb-style category navigation row. Below the navigation, a large bordered main content box holds the thread creation form and its related helpers (duplicate checker, disclaimer toggle, submission status messaging). The optional thread preview appears as a separate bordered box stacked directly beneath the main form, all left-aligned with consistent side margins.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A modest left and right gutter of about 16–24px separates the content column from the browser edges. The search bar, navigation, main form box, and preview box all span nearly the full content width (about 80–90% of the page width) but remain part of one fluid column. The main thread-creation form box occupies most of the vertical space; all other elements (search, navigation, status messages, duplicate checker, disclaimer toggle) are slim bands above or within it. The preview panel uses notable vertical space only when active, but still less than or comparable to the form itself.
layout_strategies.content_density: All components are compact and text-heavy, with tight line-height and minimal padding. Vertical gaps between major stacked sections (search strip, navigation line, main form box, preview box) are small—around 8–12px. Inside the main form, each field row sits close to the next with just a 4–8px gap, and inputs/buttons have only 2–4px internal padding. There are no decorative elements or large whitespace blocks; the layout prioritizes fitting many lines of text and form controls within a single viewport.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reading flow is strongly vertical and left-aligned. The eye starts at the page title (above), then moves slightly down into the global search strip, scanning its inline controls from left to right. It then drops to the breadcrumb-style category navigation line, again reading left to right. From there, attention is pulled down into the large bordered thread creation form, moving top to bottom through title, duplicate-thread hints, body, disclaimer toggle, and submission controls. Status messages appear near the top of the form, and the preview panel, when visible, is read last as a full-width block directly below the form. There is no side-to-side zig-zag; everything aligns to a single, stable left margin.

layout.overall_description: The page looks like an early-2000s forum "New Thread" screen: plain white background, black text, blue links, and basic HTML form controls with system-default styling. All elements sit in a single, left-aligned column with modest side gutters. At the top, a compact search strip runs horizontally with its label, text field, and button in one line, followed by a single-row breadcrumb-like navigation line. Just below, a large rectangular box with a thin gray border dominates the page; this is the main thread creation form. Inside it, labels and inputs are arranged in tight, stacked rows, resembling classic forum post forms. Directly under the title field, a narrow helper area can show potential duplicate threads, then the body textarea stretches across most of the box. Near the bottom, a simple checkbox row controls the disclaimer, followed by submit/preview actions and a small zone for status messages. When the user previews the thread, another bordered box—similar in width and style to the form—appears immediately below, showing the post as plain formatted text. The entire page feels utilitarian and compact, emphasizing function and dense information over visual decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar sits near the top of the content column, directly below the page title and above the category navigation line. It occupies a full-width horizontal strip within the content area, starting at the left gutter and extending to about 80–90% of the viewport width, with 16–24px margins to the browser edge. Inside this strip, the search label, text input, and submit button are arranged inline from left to right, resembling a traditional login/search row: label first, then a medium-width input (roughly 220–260px), then a compact button. Vertical padding inside the strip is minimal—around 4–8px top and bottom—so the row stays short and compact. There is a small vertical gap of about 8px between this strip and both the title above and the breadcrumb/navigation line below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the normal document layout as a fixed-height horizontal strip; it is not sticky or floating and simply pushes subsequent content down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category navigation controls form a single breadcrumb-style text line directly below the global search bar, aligned to the same left margin. This line spans the same usable width as the search strip (about 80–90% of page width), but its height is just that of a single line of text. Individual category names are blue links separated by simple characters (e.g., ">>" or "/"), and the entire row hugs the top of the main content column with almost no extra padding—roughly 4px above and below. There is a small 8–12px vertical gap between this navigation line and the top of the main thread-creation form box beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard inline text row within the flow, expanding horizontally as needed but remaining a single line when possible; it does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-creation-form sits immediately below the category navigation line as a large, rectangular, thinly bordered box that dominates the central part of the page. It is left-aligned with the navigation, spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, with about 8–12px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, form fields are arranged in vertically stacked rows with tight spacing (4–8px between rows), following a classic forum layout: labels or short text on the left and corresponding input fields on the right, possibly realized as simple block elements or a two-column table. The title/prefix fields appear at the top, followed directly by the duplicate-thread-checker area, then the large body textarea taking up several text lines in height—this textarea occupies the majority of the form’s vertical space. Below the body, additional controls like the disclaimer toggle and action buttons are shown as short, single-line rows. Submission status messages appear near the top or just above the buttons within this same bordered box, keeping all thread-creation interactions visually contained in one main panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically to fit its contents while maintaining a consistent full-width presence; it scrolls with the page as a single block and does not have fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duplicate-thread-checker is embedded inside the thread-creation-form, positioned directly beneath the thread title input row and aligned with the input column. It appears as a narrow horizontal band or small block of text within the form’s border, using the same left padding as the other field content. When inactive, it may be a single compact line of faint instructional text or remain nearly invisible with only a small vertical gap. When active, it lists potential duplicate threads as one or more single-line blue links, stacked tightly with minimal 2–4px spacing between them, all in the same narrow column under the title. There is no separate border around this area; it feels like an integrated helper row between title and body, increasing the panel’s height slightly but not breaking the dense, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the form’s normal flow, expanding vertically only as needed to show results and pushing the body textarea and subsequent fields downward as its content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The disclaimer-toggle-panel sits near the lower part of the thread-creation-form, beneath the main body textarea and above the submission actions. It is presented as a single compact row aligned with the form’s input column, containing a checkbox (or similar control) immediately followed by its text label on the same line. The left edge of the checkbox is aligned with other input fields, with only 2–4px of internal padding around the controls. The vertical spacing between this row and the body textarea above, as well as the submit/preview controls below, is tight—roughly 4–8px—so the panel reads as a small inline option rather than a major section break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a single normal row in the form’s vertical stack, with fixed height determined by its text line and no special positioning or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-preview-panel appears as a separate bordered box directly beneath the main thread-creation-form, sharing the same left alignment, width (about 80–90% of the page), and side margins. There is a small, consistent vertical gap of about 8–12px between the bottom of the form and the top of the preview box to visually separate editing from viewing. Inside the panel, content is presented as a simple text preview of the resulting thread: a bold title line at the top, followed by body content rendered in standard forum text style with tight line spacing and minimal padding (around 8px on all sides). The preview’s vertical size grows naturally with the length of the content, but the overall feel remains compact and text-heavy, with no extra chrome beyond the thin border and basic text formatting.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The panel is hidden or absent until the user invokes a preview; when present, it occupies a full-width block in the normal document flow beneath the form, scrolling as part of the page with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-handler manifests as a slim message area inside the top region of the thread-creation-form box, typically just below the form’s internal header (if present) or directly above the first input row. It aligns with the same left padding as the other form content and usually occupies a single or small number of text lines. Success, error, or in-progress messages appear as plain text, possibly with simple color emphasis (e.g., red for errors, green for success), but with no additional visual framing beyond the form’s existing border. Vertical padding around these messages is minimal—about 4px above and below—so they appear tightly integrated into the form. When no status is active, this area shrinks away entirely or reduces to a near-zero-height gap, keeping the layout dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the form’s normal vertical flow, temporarily occupying space and shifting subsequent fields downward when a message is shown; it is not fixed and does not overlay other content.

name: User Profile
title: User Profile
description: Displays a community member’s profile information, recent HYIP program comments, and forum activity, and lets other users search the site or start a private message with this member.

core_features: Load and display a community member’s profile using the profileId URL parameter via getCommunityProfile.
Show basic user information including username, join date, bio (if available), aggregate activity counts, and last active time.
Display a list of the user’s recent program comments with timestamps and links to the related program detail pages.
Display a list of the user’s recent forum posts with timestamps and links to the related forum thread pages.
Allow switching between viewing recent program comments and recent forum posts (activity focus) on the profile.
Provide a 'Send Private Message' action that navigates to message_compose.html with the recipientProfileId set from the loaded profile.
Provide a global search bar on the page that calls searchSite for inline results preview and can direct users to a dedicated search results page.
user_workflows:
View another member’s profile:
1) User clicks a username link on program_detail.html, forum_thread.html, or search_results.html.
2) Browser navigates to user_profile.html with the profileId URL parameter set to the selected CommunityProfile.id.
3) On page load, the profile-data-loader component reads profileId from the URL and calls getCommunityProfile(profileId).
4) When the response returns, profile-header displays the CommunityProfile data; activity components render recent_program_comments and recent_forum_posts.
5) User can switch between comments and posts views using the activity switcher without leaving the page.

Send a private message from the profile:
1) User opens user_profile.html for a given profileId as above.
2) After getCommunityProfile completes, the send-private-message-button is enabled and bound to the loaded profile.id.
3) User clicks the 'Send Private Message' button.
4) Browser navigates to message_compose.html, adding recipientProfileId=<profile.id> as a URL parameter.
5) The compose page (not part of this spec) uses that parameter to pre-fill the recipient.

Inspect a program referenced in the user’s recent comments:
1) User is on user_profile.html with the profile already loaded.
2) In the recent-program-comments-list, each comment shows a clickable program-related label bound to comment.program_id.
3) User clicks this program label.
4) Browser navigates to program_detail.html with id=<comment.program_id> as a URL parameter, allowing the user to view that HYIP’s details and the full comment thread there.

Inspect a thread referenced in the user’s recent forum posts:
1) User is on user_profile.html with the profile already loaded.
2) In the recent-forum-posts-list, each post shows a clickable thread-related label bound to post.thread_id.
3) User clicks this thread label.
4) Browser navigates to forum_thread.html with threadId=<post.thread_id> as a URL parameter, allowing the user to view the full discussion.

Search from the user profile page:
1) User types a keyword into the global-search-bar.
2) As the query changes, the component may optionally debounce and call searchSite(query, type_filter, page, page_size) to show inline preview results for programs and forum threads.
3) User reviews the inline preview results beneath the search field (if implemented) and can click any program or thread result to navigate to the appropriate detail page using their IDs.
4) Alternatively, the user submits the search form, which can redirect the browser to search_results.html with query and optional type_filter in the URL parameters for a full search experience.
interactions: On initial page load or when the profileId URL parameter changes, the profile-data-loader calls getCommunityProfile with the current profileId and updates bound components with the response.
If profileId is missing or invalid, an error state is shown in a profile-error-state component and data-dependent components do not render user-specific content.
Clicking the activity switcher controls (e.g., 'Program Comments' or 'Forum Posts') toggles which activity list is visible and may update an activity URL parameter to reflect the chosen view.
Clicking the 'Send Private Message' button triggers navigation to message_compose.html with recipientProfileId set to the loaded profile.id.
Clicking on a program-related label in the recent program comments list triggers navigation to program_detail.html with id set to the comment.program_id of the clicked item.
Clicking on a thread-related label in the recent forum posts list triggers navigation to forum_thread.html with threadId set to the post.thread_id of the clicked item.
Entering text in the global-search-bar updates the internal search query state; optionally, a debounced change handler calls searchSite to fetch preview results.
Submitting the search form either executes searchSite and shows richer inline results or redirects the user to search_results.html with query and optional type_filter in the URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters. The required profileId parameter determines which CommunityProfile is loaded. On every load and when profileId changes, getCommunityProfile(profileId) is executed; all profile-bound components listen to this data. If profileId is absent, the page shows an error and does not attempt the API call. An optional activity parameter can store the currently selected activity view (e.g., 'comments' or 'posts'); the activity switcher reads this parameter on initialization to decide which list to display and updates it when the user switches views, allowing deep-linking directly to a particular activity tab. Search input state for the global search bar is managed within that component; the query is only persisted to the URL when the user is redirected to search_results.html (e.g., by setting query and type_filter parameters there). No profile-specific or activity-specific state is stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence; all restorable state is encoded in URL parameters, and all user-specific content is re-fetched from getCommunityProfile using the current profileId when the page is loaded or reloaded.

components.functionality: Reads the profileId from the user_profile.html URL, calls getCommunityProfile with that ID, and exposes the resulting profile, recent_program_comments, and recent_forum_posts data to other components. Handles loading and error states related to fetching the community profile.

Displays core information about the community member whose profile is loaded, including username, join date, avatar (if available), bio, total comment/thread counts, and last active timestamp. It is read-only and depends entirely on the data provided by profile-data-loader.

Allows the user to switch between viewing the member’s recent program comments and recent forum posts. Reads an optional activity URL parameter on initialization (such as 'comments' or 'posts') to set the active tab and updates that parameter when the user switches tabs. Controls which activity list components are visible based on the active tab.

Shows the user’s recent program-related comments returned by getCommunityProfile.recent_program_comments. For each ProgramComment, displays author_username, created_at, edited_at (if available), rating_stars and is_review (if present), and the comment body. Includes a clickable program-related label linked to comment.program_id that navigates to the corresponding program_detail.html page.

Shows the user’s recent forum posts returned by getCommunityProfile.recent_forum_posts. For each ForumPost, displays author_username, created_at, edited_at (if available), an excerpt or full body of the post, and a clickable thread-related label that uses post.thread_id for navigation to forum_thread.html.

Renders a 'Send Private Message' control when a valid CommunityProfile is loaded. When clicked, it navigates to message_compose.html with recipientProfileId set to the current profile.id. The button is disabled or hidden while profile data is loading or if there is an error loading the profile.

Provides a site-wide search input at the top of the profile page. Accepts a query string and an optional type_filter (e.g., to limit to programs or forum threads). On input changes, it can call searchSite(query, type_filter, page, page_size) to fetch preview search results. On submit, it may either update inline results or redirect to search_results.html with the query and type filter encoded in the URL parameters.

Optionally displays a concise list of search results returned by the global-search-bar’s searchSite calls. Shows separate sections for program_results and forum_thread_results, each with clickable items that navigate to the corresponding program_detail.html or forum_thread.html using the IDs contained in the result items.

Displays error messages when the profile cannot be loaded, such as when profileId is missing, malformed, or when getCommunityProfile returns an error. Suppresses or overlays other profile-specific components when in an error state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column stack of sections, mirroring the mockup’s sequence of title → utility strip → breadcrumb → main list box. At the top is the profile header (serving as the page title area). Directly beneath that, a single horizontal utility strip is dedicated to the global search form. Below the search strip, a narrow band is reserved for inline search results when present. Next comes a single-line tab-like controller acting as the breadcrumb analogue, and finally a large bordered content box fills most of the remaining vertical space, switching between the two activity lists (comments vs. forum posts). Supporting states like loader and error messages appear inline within the main content box without changing this overall stacking order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column stack of sections, mirroring the mockup’s sequence of title → utility strip → breadcrumb → main list box. At the top is the profile header (serving as the page title area). Directly beneath that, a single horizontal utility strip is dedicated to the global search form. Below the search strip, a narrow band is reserved for inline search results when present. Next comes a single-line tab-like controller acting as the breadcrumb analogue, and finally a large bordered content box fills most of the remaining vertical space, switching between the two activity lists (comments vs. forum posts). Supporting states like loader and error messages appear inline within the main content box without changing this overall stacking order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Moderate side margins of roughly 16–24px separate the content from the browser edges. Vertically, the top sections (profile header, search bar, activity switcher) are relatively compact and consume only about 15–20% of the initial viewport height combined, echoing the mockup. The main bordered content box occupies the bulk of the page—around 70–80% of the viewport height on a typical screen—so that recent comments/posts dominate the view. Each section is separated by small vertical gaps of 8–12px, with the main content box having 8–12px internal padding on all sides. Inline elements (inputs, buttons, tabs) have only 2–4px internal padding and minimal margins.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text and controls are dense and compact, with relatively tight line-height and minimal vertical spacing between lines of text or list items. Lists of comments and forum posts are stacked with small gaps (around 4–8px), favoring the display of as many entries as possible per viewport. There are no decorative blocks, large banners, or oversized headings; all elements resemble default HTML styling with just basic borders and spacing, keeping the page information-heavy and visually utilitarian.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye begins at the top-left with the bold username and profile summary, then naturally shifts slightly downward to the search strip. From the search field and button, the eye moves down to any inline search results, then to the tab-like activity switcher that announces which content view is active. The main reading flow leads straight down into the large bordered box, where the user scans a vertical list of entries. Within each list, scanning is left-to-right per line, then quickly downward to the next item, preserving the strong vertical reading flow seen in the mockup. State messages (loading, errors) appear in the same main content area, so they are noticed without disrupting this straightforward top-down flow.

layout.overall_description: The user profile page reads like a classic early-2000s forum profile: a plain white background with a single, left-aligned column of content framed by modest side margins. At the very top-left, the member’s username and basic profile details appear in a compact block that functions as the page header. Immediately underneath, a simple text-based search form sits on its own horizontal strip, with a text input and submit button laid out inline. When a search is executed, a narrow, lightly bordered panel of search results appears directly below this strip, pushing the rest of the content downward but staying within the same linear flow. Beneath that, a one-line activity switcher, styled like simple text links in a breadcrumb row, lets the user toggle between “Program Comments” and “Forum Posts.” Most of the screen is taken up by a rectangular bordered box spanning almost the full content width and stretching down the page. Inside this box, one of the two activity lists is shown at a time: a dense list of recent HYIP program comments or recent forum posts, each entry rendered as a compact line or two of plain text and links. When profile data is loading or errors occur, small inline messages appear at the top of this box instead of the lists. The overall impression is a strictly functional, text-first layout with minimal ornamentation, where the main content list dominates and everything else quietly supports it from above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile data loader occupies space only within the main bordered content box, in the same area where the activity lists normally appear. When active (e.g., while fetching profile and activity data), it appears as a single line or short block of plain text—such as “Loading profile…”—aligned to the left inside the box. It sits flush with the box’s top padding, roughly 8–12px below the box’s top border and 8–12px from the left border, matching the internal padding used for list content. No overlay or modal behavior is used; the loader simply replaces the list content while leaving the overall page structure (header, search bar, tabs) unchanged. As soon as data is loaded, this text disappears and the relevant list fills the rest of the box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content box; appears at the top of that box and temporarily replaces the lists without affecting the placement or size of other page sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile header is the first content block at the top-left of the page, directly under any site-level navigation (not part of this layout). It spans nearly the full width of the content column, starting about 16–24px from the left browser edge and ending with a similar margin on the right. Vertically, it is compact—roughly 48–80px high depending on the amount of profile info shown. The top line contains the member’s username in slightly bolder or larger default text, aligned left. Directly below that, one or two lines of secondary details (join date, status, last active) are stacked with tight line-height. The send-private-message button is placed on the same top line as the username, aligned toward the right edge of this header block: the username starts at the left, and the button is positioned toward the far right, leaving a flexible gap between them so the layout can adapt to wider or narrower screens. If the viewport is narrow, the PM button can wrap to a new line beneath the username but remains visually part of the header block. Small vertical spacing (around 8px) separates the header from the search strip below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section at the top of the profile page; height grows with profile text but it does not scroll independently or stick—it scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity switcher appears as a single thin horizontal band below the inline search results panel (or directly below the search bar when no results are visible). It is left-aligned and spans the same width as the rest of the content column, with roughly 16–24px margins from the browser edges. Inside this band, the available activity views—such as “Program Comments” and “Forum Posts”—are rendered as simple text links laid out inline on the left, separated by a minimal delimiter like a vertical bar or a small amount of whitespace. The font size matches or is slightly smaller than body text, mimicking a breadcrumb line. There is no tabbed chrome or heavy styling—only perhaps an underline or bold treatment on the active view’s link. A small vertical gap of about 8px separates this switcher from the top edge of the main bordered content box beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block in the normal flow; toggling between tabs does not move the switcher itself, but changes which list is shown inside the main content box below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent program comments list fills the main bordered content box when the corresponding activity view is active. This box begins just below the activity switcher, with approximately 8–12px of separation, and spans almost the entire content width, leaving 16–24px left and right margins from the browser edges. The box itself has a simple 1px light gray border and 8–12px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, the comments are arranged as a vertical stack of entries with high density: each entry is rendered as one or two lines of text, aligned left, running nearly the full width of the box. A typical entry starts at the left with a small, possibly numbered label or timestamp, followed by the program name as a blue text link, then a hyphen and a domain or thread link in blue, and then any brief comment snippet or meta information in plain or slightly grayed text. The vertical spacing between entries is minimal—around 4–8px—so many items can be seen without scrolling. The list extends downward, increasing the box’s height as needed and driving page scroll; there is no internal scrolling container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the full interior width of the main content box when active; appears in the normal flow and is mutually exclusive with the forum posts list (only one list is visible in the box at a time).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent forum posts list occupies the exact same spatial slot as the program comments list, inside the same main bordered content box, but only when its activity view is selected in the switcher. It shares the same 1px light gray box border and 8–12px internal padding. Entries are laid out as a dense vertical stack of text lines aligned to the left. Each entry typically begins with a small label (e.g., forum section or timestamp), followed by the topic title as a blue link, then possibly a hyphen and a secondary link or metadata (such as the thread category), and a brief gray parenthetical note with post count or reply count. The layout is very similar to a classic forum topic list: tight line-height, minimal vertical gap of 4–8px between items, and no icons or decorative bullets beyond simple list semantics if used. The list extends downward, making the box grow in height and causing the main page scroll, mirroring the highly vertical flow of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the main content box area with the comments list; only one list is rendered at a time based on the active activity switcher selection, with no change to the position or size of the surrounding box.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The send-private-message button is positioned within the profile header block, aligned toward the top-right of that block so it is immediately discoverable but still unobtrusive. Horizontally, the username appears at the top-left while this button sits on the same line at the far right, with flexible space between. The button uses a simple default HTML style (a small rectangular button or a plain text link styled to look like a button) with 2–4px internal padding and little to no extra outer margin. Vertically, it aligns with the baseline of the username text. If screen width is limited, the button wraps to a new line directly below the username, maintaining a small 4–8px gap from the text above. There is no dedicated row or oversized call-to-action styling—this remains a compact utility control within the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the profile header content; its presence does not change the stacking order of main sections, and it scrolls away with the header as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar occupies a full-width horizontal strip directly below the profile header, separated from it by a small vertical gap of about 8px. It is left-aligned within the same content column, with approximately 16–24px margins from the left and right browser edges, matching the header. The strip itself is visually simple: a single line containing a text input field and a submit button arranged inline. The text input spans roughly 40–60% of the content width but hugs the left side, while the button appears immediately to the right of the input with only a small 4px gap between them. Both the input and button use minimal padding (2–4px inside) and default browser styling, echoing an early-2000s form appearance. There is a small 8–12px vertical gap beneath this strip before any inline search results appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard form strip in the normal document flow; as more content is added below (like results or the main box), it remains fixed in place and scrolls away with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline search results panel is placed directly beneath the global search bar, with a small 8–12px vertical gap separating it from the search strip above. It shares the same horizontal alignment and width as the rest of the content column, leaving approximately 16–24px margins on the left and right. When visible, the panel appears as a compact, lightly bordered rectangle (1px light gray border) with 8–12px internal padding. Inside, search results are presented as a tight vertical list of plain text lines and blue links, similar in density to the main activity lists but typically shorter in height. Each result line is left-aligned, with minimal spacing (4–8px) between results. The panel’s height expands as needed to fit the results and pushes the activity switcher and main content box down accordingly. When there are no results or when the user closes it, the entire panel collapses and the activity switcher moves up to sit directly beneath the search bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the flow; appears and disappears without overlaying other content, simply shifting the sections below it up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile error state message is shown within the main bordered content box, occupying the top part of that box where the lists or loader would otherwise appear. When active, the box still appears with its normal 1px light gray border and 8–12px internal padding. The error text sits at the top-left of the box, roughly 8–12px below the top border and 8–12px from the left border, and spans a single line or short paragraph in slightly emphasized styling (for example, red or dark text, possibly bold). There is minimal additional spacing inside this message—just enough to keep it easily readable. No extra panel or overlay is used; the error message simply replaces the list or loader content while preserving the surrounding layout (header, search bar, activity switcher).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content box; appears instead of normal list content when an error occurs, without changing the size or position of the overall box or other page sections.

name: Compose Message
title: Compose Message
description: Page for composing and sending a private message to another community member about HYIP programs, with optional context about a specific program and comment.

core_features: Read URL parameters (recipientProfileId, relatedProgramId, relatedCommentId) and initialize the compose form with the correct recipient and contextual metadata.
Display the target recipient’s username and profile link, prefilled based on recipientProfileId.
Optionally display contextual information about the related HYIP program and/or program comment when relatedProgramId and/or relatedCommentId are provided.
Provide a form to enter a message subject and body with validation (required fields, basic length checks).
Call sendPrivateMessage on form submission to send the private message including contextual IDs when available.
Show success or error feedback based on the sendPrivateMessage response and, on success, provide navigation back to the recipient’s profile.
Provide a global search input that uses searchSite to search programs and forum threads (e.g., for quick navigation while composing).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open compose page from a user profile
1. User clicks an action (e.g., 'Send Private Message') on user_profile.html.
2. Browser navigates to message_compose.html with recipientProfileId in the URL.
3. The page reads recipientProfileId from the URL and resolves the recipient’s CommunityProfile data server-side.
4. The recipient info panel shows the resolved username and a link back to user_profile.html?profileId=recipientProfileId.
5. The compose form displays with the recipient field locked or non-editable, and empty subject and body fields (unless the server pre-populates them).
6. User enters a subject and message body.
7. User clicks the send button, triggering form submission and a sendPrivateMessage call with recipientProfileId, subject, body, and undefined relatedProgramId/relatedCommentId.
8. On success, the page shows a success confirmation and a link/button to return to user_profile.html?profileId=recipientProfileId; optionally, the page may auto-redirect after a short delay.
9. On error, the page displays the error message from sendPrivateMessage without clearing the subject/body so the user can correct and resend.

Workflow: Open compose page from a program comment
1. User is on program_detail.html viewing a specific program and its comments.
2. User clicks an action to privately contact a commenter, which navigates to message_compose.html with recipientProfileId, relatedProgramId, and relatedCommentId in the URL.
3. The page reads all three URL parameters and server-side logic resolves the recipient CommunityProfile, Program, and ProgramComment for display.
4. The context panel shows program information and a short excerpt or reference to the related comment to remind the sender what they are following up on.
5. The recipient info panel shows the recipient’s username.
6. The compose form may be pre-populated with a contextual subject based on the related program/comment (handled server-side), while body starts empty.
7. User edits or fills in the subject and body, then submits the form.
8. The page calls sendPrivateMessage with recipientProfileId, subject, body, relatedProgramId, and relatedCommentId.
9. If sendPrivateMessage returns success, the page shows a confirmation and provides options to go back to the program_detail.html page (handled by browser back button) and/or to the recipient’s user_profile.html.
10. If sendPrivateMessage returns an error, the error is shown and inputs remain so the user can retry.

Workflow: Use global search while composing
1. While on message_compose.html, the user types a keyword into the global search input in the header.
2. On submit, the component either:
   - Calls searchSite to fetch matching Program and ForumThread results to display in an inline results panel on the same page, or
   - Navigates to a dedicated search results page with query parameters (handled outside this page’s scope) after optional pre-validation.
3. If inline results are shown, the user can click a result to open it in a new tab, keeping their in-progress message intact on message_compose.html.
4. The compose form state remains unchanged while searchSite is used, allowing the user to resume editing and then send the message.
interactions: On initial page load, the page controller reads recipientProfileId, relatedProgramId, and relatedCommentId from the URL query string and uses them to initialize the recipient info panel, contextual info panel, and hidden fields in the compose form.
If recipientProfileId is missing or invalid, the compose form is disabled and an error state is shown indicating that a recipient is required; the send button remains inactive.
Typing in the subject input updates the internal form state for subject; basic validation flags an empty subject when the user attempts to submit.
Typing in the body textarea updates the internal form state for body; basic validation flags an empty body when the user attempts to submit.

Clicking the send button triggers onSubmit on the compose form, which performs client-side validation; if validation passes, it calls sendPrivateMessage with the current form values and contextual IDs from URL/state.
While sendPrivateMessage is in-flight, the send button is disabled and an in-progress state is shown to prevent duplicate submissions.
If sendPrivateMessage returns success: true, the success panel is displayed, the form may be cleared or locked, and a link/button is provided to go to user_profile.html?profileId=recipientProfileId.
If sendPrivateMessage returns success: false, an error panel shows the message from the response; the form remains populated so the user can adjust and resubmit.
Clicking the recipient’s username in the recipient info panel takes the user to user_profile.html?profileId=recipientProfileId in the same tab or a new tab, depending on implementation.

Typing into the global search input and submitting triggers searchSite with the entered query, selected filter (if any), and paging parameters; search results are then handled within the search component (either inline or via navigation to a search results page).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is driven by URL query parameters and in-memory form state, without using localStorage.

URL parameter handling:
- recipientProfileId (required for sending):
  - On page initialization, the script reads recipientProfileId from the query string.
  - If present, it is stored in the page state and bound as a hidden field in the compose form.
  - Server-side rendering or a pre-fetch layer uses this ID to populate CommunityProfile data for display (username, profile link). The client-side script does not call any additional interfaces beyond the assigned ones.
  - If absent or determined invalid by the server, the page enters an error state where the compose form is disabled and a message indicates that a valid recipient is required.

- relatedProgramId (optional):
  - If present in the query string, it is stored in page state and bound to hidden fields in the compose form so sendPrivateMessage includes it.
  - Server-side may use this ID to resolve and inject Program data into the initial HTML, which is then bound to the context panel.
  - If not present, the context panel omits program-related information.

- relatedCommentId (optional):
  - If present, it is stored similarly and bound as a hidden field in the compose form for sendPrivateMessage.
  - Server-side may use it to resolve ProgramComment data for display (e.g., excerpt) inside the context panel.
  - If not present, comment-specific context is hidden.

Form and interaction state:
- Subject and body fields are kept in transient page state (e.g., component state or form values). They are not persisted to the URL.
- Validation state (e.g., subjectMissing, bodyMissing) is also maintained in transient state and is reset when the user edits the respective fields.
- A sending state flag (e.g., isSending) controls the disabled/enabled state of the send button and the display of progress feedback.
- A sendResult state (e.g., success or error) is set based on sendPrivateMessage response. On success, sendResult holds the returned message_record for potential use (for example, to show the timestamp), but this is not required for core navigation.

Navigation logic:
- After a successful send, the page displays a link that navigates to user_profile.html?profileId=recipientProfileId. This uses the value from the initial URL/state rather than any hardcoded profile identifier.
- The browser back button can return the user to the source page (user_profile.html or program_detail.html) using the standard history. The compose page itself does not modify the URL beyond the initial parameters.

Global search state:
- The global search input maintains its own transient query string and optional type_filter and paging state.
- Submitting search triggers searchSite with the exact values from the inputs; results are either shown inline or trigger navigation to another page using URL parameters (e.g., search.html?query=...), but this page’s own URL parameters for messaging are not modified.

components.functionality: Provides a global search field in the header for searching across HYIP programs and forum threads while on the compose message page. It uses the searchSite interface to retrieve results based on the entered query and optional filters.

Displays the target recipient’s basic info, including username and a link to their profile, based on recipientProfileId from the URL. Indicates that the message will be sent to this user and ensures the recipient cannot be changed from this page.

Shows contextual information about the related program and/or comment that led to this private message, when relatedProgramId and/or relatedCommentId are present. If either parameter is missing, the corresponding contextual section is hidden.

Main form for composing the private message. It includes a read-only recipient display (synced with recipient-info-panel), editable subject and body fields, hidden bindings for recipientProfileId, relatedProgramId, and relatedCommentId, validation logic, and submission via sendPrivateMessage.

Displays success or error messages after attempting to send a private message. On success, it may show details from the returned message_record and provides navigation back to the recipient’s profile using recipientProfileId. On error, it shows the error message and keeps the form active for corrections.

Aggregates and displays client-side validation messages for the subject and body fields (e.g., required field messages). It is updated when the user interacts with the compose form and when onSubmit is triggered.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom, single-column flow aligned to the left, similar to an old-school forum. At the very top is the bold page title (outside this spec), followed directly by a narrow horizontal strip for the global search bar. Below that would be the breadcrumb (global, not a component here). The main content is a single large bordered compose box that fills most of the page height. Inside that box, information and controls are stacked in this order: recipient info panel at the top, then the message context panel, then validation messages (when present), then the main message compose fields, and finally the send status feedback at the very bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom, single-column flow aligned to the left, similar to an old-school forum. At the very top is the bold page title (outside this spec), followed directly by a narrow horizontal strip for the global search bar. Below that would be the breadcrumb (global, not a component here). The main content is a single large bordered compose box that fills most of the page height. Inside that box, information and controls are stacked in this order: recipient info panel at the top, then the message context panel, then validation messages (when present), then the main message compose fields, and finally the send status feedback at the very bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The outer page has modest left and right margins of about 16–24px from the browser edge, with all components constrained within this main content column. The global search bar occupies only a thin horizontal band, roughly 5–10% of the vertical viewport. The bordered compose form container spans nearly the full available width (minus the page margins) and takes up the majority of the vertical space, similar to the large list box in the mockup. Within the compose box, narrow vertical gaps of about 8px separate the internal panels (recipient info, context, validation, form fields, send status) to keep the stack compact but readable.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally dense, with tight line-height and minimal padding. The compose container has about 8–12px of internal padding on all sides. Inline elements like text inputs, textareas, and buttons have very small internal padding (2–4px) and are tightly stacked with 4–8px gaps between rows. Text blocks (recipient info, context, messages) sit close together, emphasizing fitting as much information as possible into the viewport while remaining legible, mimicking a utilitarian early-2000s forum style.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the bold 'Compose Message' title at the top left, then moves horizontally across the global search strip as the eye notices the label, search input, and button in a single row. From there, the eye drops down along the breadcrumb line (global) and then into the main bordered compose box. Inside the box, reading follows a straightforward vertical path: first the recipient info line, then the contextual program note, then any validation messages, then the main message fields (subject and body), and finally the submit controls and any send status feedback. Within each horizontal row, the eye scans left to right from labels to input fields, with no decorative distractions.

layout.overall_description: The page feels like an old-school investment forum utility screen: everything sits on a plain white background with a narrow left margin and a single tall column of content marching straight down. At the very top, a bold text title announces 'Compose Message', followed immediately by a low-profile horizontal strip for search that blends in with default browser form styling. Beneath this, the user would see the breadcrumb trail, and then their attention is drawn into a large rectangular box outlined with a thin gray border that dominates the rest of the page height – this is the compose area, analogous to the main list box in the mockup. Inside this box, simple text lines and basic form controls stack in tight vertical succession: first who the message is going to, then a concise program/context line in smaller or grayer text, followed by any red validation messages if something is wrong. Directly under that, the main message fields (subject and a large textarea) occupy most of the vertical space, with compact labels and small default-style inputs. At the bottom of this same box, small, plain buttons for sending are followed by a single line of green or red feedback text when a message is sent or fails. The overall impression is functional, compact, and text-driven, with no visual embellishments beyond simple borders and font weight to guide the eye down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar sits near the top of the main content column, directly below the bold page title and above the breadcrumb line. It occupies a single horizontal strip that spans nearly the full content width (roughly 90–100% of the available width inside the page margins), but its elements are compact and aligned slightly to the right within that strip, echoing the login-style row from the mockup. Within this strip, a short text label ('Search') appears on the left of the search control group, followed immediately by a single-line text input that takes up about 40–50% of the row width, and then a small default-looking submit button to its right; all three are inline and vertically middle-aligned. Vertical padding above and below this strip is minimal (about 8px total), so it reads as a thin band of functionality before the main compose box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block-level row in the normal document flow, expanding horizontally with the viewport but constrained by the page’s left and right margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipient info panel is positioned inside the main compose area at the very top of the bordered compose box, forming the first row of content within that container. It spans the full internal width of the box, with about 8–12px of horizontal padding from the left and right edges of the border. The content appears as a single text line starting at the left, using a simple label like 'To:' in bold followed by the recipient username in regular-weight text (possibly a blue link if profile navigation is desired), all on one line. There is only a small vertical gap (about 4–8px) between this panel and the next (message context), emphasizing it as part of a continuous top-down reading sequence rather than a visually separated card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that sits as the first row inside the private-message-compose-form container, moving with it and resizing as the container width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message context panel appears directly beneath the recipient info line inside the same bordered compose box, separated by a small vertical gap of about 4–8px. It also spans the full internal width of the container, aligned to the left with the same 8–12px padding from the border. Its content is denser and visually subtler: a single line or two of smaller or slightly gray text summarizing the context, such as 'Regarding: [Program Name link] – [Program Domain link] (comment: "short excerpt")'. Program name and domain are plain blue text links, while any parenthetical note or comment snippet is rendered in a slightly lighter gray to echo the 'number of replies' style from the mockup. The panel feels like an inline textual hint rather than a separate box, keeping the main vertical flow uninterrupted as the eye moves down to the form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-text block within the private-message-compose-form container, wrapping to one or two lines as needed but never breaking out of the main column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The private message compose form is the main bordered container that dominates the central area of the page. Positioned below the breadcrumb (global) and spanning almost the full width of the content column (leaving about 16–24px margin to the browser edges), it is outlined with a thin gray border and has internal padding of roughly 8–12px on all sides. Inside, content is arranged in a tightly stacked vertical sequence: first the recipient-info-panel at the top, then the message-context-panel, followed by any form-validation-messages, then the core form fields (a 'Subject' label with a single-line text input on its own row, and a 'Message' label above or to the left of a multi-line textarea that occupies most of the remaining height), and finally a row of small default-style buttons (e.g., 'Send', 'Preview', 'Cancel') right-aligned or left-aligned near the bottom. Vertical spacing between these internal rows is kept compact (4–8px), and all inputs use default browser styling with minimal internal padding, reinforcing the utilitarian forum feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in the normal document flow, expanding vertically with content and horizontally with the viewport up to the page’s margins, serving as the primary structural frame for the other panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The send status panel is located inside the private-message-compose-form container at the very bottom of the internal content stack, directly beneath the row of submit buttons. It spans the full internal width of the form and is left-aligned with the rest of the text, sitting only 4–8px below the buttons so it feels tightly associated with the action just taken. When visible (after a send attempt), it typically displays a single line of feedback text such as 'Message sent successfully.' in a slightly bold green or 'Failed to send message.' in red; the styling remains minimal with no background color blocks or icons, just a color change and possibly a slight font-weight change. The panel’s height is minimal, collapsing to zero or being omitted entirely when there is no status to show, thus not introducing unnecessary whitespace in the dense layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered static block within the compose form, appearing and disappearing in place without affecting the positioning of components above it beyond normal reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form validation messages panel is placed inside the private-message-compose-form container between the message-context-panel and the first main input field row (usually the Subject field). It occupies the full internal width of the compose box and starts flush with the left padding, keeping alignment consistent with labels and text above. When there are validation errors, it shows as a tight vertical stack of one or more short lines (e.g., bullet-less text like 'Subject is required.' or 'Message body must be at least 20 characters.'), rendered in a red text color with perhaps slightly smaller font size but normal weight, echoing early forum error styles. The vertical spacing between these lines is very small (around 4px), and the gap between this panel and the form fields below is also minimal (4–8px), so the errors feel directly attached to the fields they reference without any bold visual framing or background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered static block within the compose form’s normal flow; appears immediately above the form fields when errors are present, pushing the fields down slightly but maintaining the overall compact, single-column structure.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays HYIP program and forum thread results for a given search query, with controls to switch between result types and paginate through matches.

core_features: Execute a unified search across HYIP programs and forum threads using the searchSite interface, based on the query URL parameter.
Display the current search query and allow users to refine it directly on the page, updating URL parameters and re-running the search.
Show tab-like controls to filter results by type_filter URL parameter: all, programs, or threads.
List HYIP program results (Program entities) with key metrics and link each item to program_detail.html via its id.
List forum thread results (ForumThread entities) with key metadata and link each item to forum_thread.html via its id.
Show total result counts for programs and threads using total_program_results and total_forum_thread_results from searchSite.
Provide pagination based on page and page_size URL parameters, updating the searchSite call whenever these change.
Handle empty search states (no query) and no-result cases (zero programs and threads) with clear functional messages.
Handle loading and error states for the searchSite call, allowing a retry without losing the current URL state.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Run a search from another page
1. User enters a search term in the global header search bar on any page.
2. The global search form submits to search_results.html with query set to the entered term and type_filter, page, page_size initialized according to site-wide defaults.
3. search_results.html reads query, type_filter, page, and page_size from the URL and calls searchSite with those parameters.
4. When searchSite returns, the page displays program_results and forum_thread_results lists, along with total counts.
5. User clicks any program result item to navigate to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}, or clicks any thread result item to navigate to forum_thread.html?threadId={ForumThread.id}.

Workflow 2: Refine query within Search Results
1. User is on search_results.html with an existing query.
2. User edits the query value in the page’s search input and submits.
3. The page updates the URL to search_results.html?query={newQuery}&type_filter=all&page=1&page_size={currentOrDefaultPageSize}.
4. The page re-calls searchSite with the new parameters and refreshes the lists and counts.
5. User can repeat this process until satisfied with the results.

Workflow 3: Switch between All / Programs / Threads
1. User is viewing results with a current query and any type_filter value.
2. User clicks the 'Programs' tab control.
3. The page updates the URL to set type_filter=programs and reset page=1 (page_size preserved), then re-calls searchSite.
4. The page displays only program_results list and total_program_results-driven pagination.
5. User clicks the 'Threads' tab; the URL changes to type_filter=threads&page=1 and searchSite is called again, now displaying only forum_thread_results.
6. User clicks the 'All' tab; the URL changes to type_filter=all&page=1, and the page shows both program_results and forum_thread_results sections using the same searchSite response.

Workflow 4: Paginate results
1. User is on any type_filter view with results displayed.
2. User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or a numbered page).
