The bordered box itself is centered in the page, starting approximately 24–32px below the breadcrumb, with about 16–24px margin from the left and right edges of the viewport. The box spans roughly 80–90% of the available width. Inside the box, the static text is aligned to the left edge with about 8–12px padding from the border on all sides. The static content forms a compact block of paragraphs and short headings, taking up roughly the top 30–40% of the box’s height on a typical viewport. Paragraphs are stacked closely with small vertical gaps (~8px) and no extra decorative spacing. There is no column split; all text flows in a single column from top to bottom. At the bottom of this static section, a small vertical gap (~8px) separates the last paragraph from the contact form fields that follow, keeping both within the same visual container while clearly indicating the transition from informational text to interactive form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element participating in normal document flow, anchored at the top of the main bordered container with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form begins immediately below the static contact content, inside the same bordered box, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 8px. It spans nearly the full internal width of the box, starting about 8–12px from the left inner edge and ending about 8–12px from the right inner edge. The form is structured as a vertical stack of rows, each row typically containing a left-aligned text label and a right-aligned input control (text field, textarea, select, etc.) on the same horizontal line, reminiscent of simple HTML table or label–input pairs. Labels occupy about 25–30% of the inner width on the left, right-aligned or closely snug to their inputs, and inputs occupy the remaining 70–75% to the right. The vertical spacing between rows is tight (~4–8px), maintaining a high information density and allowing multiple fields to be visible without scrolling. Multi-line fields (such as a message textarea) extend downward but still honor the compact spacing above and below. The submit button sits at the bottom-left of the form area, aligned with the left edge of the input column (not centered across the page), with only a small top margin (~8px) from the last input field. Overall, the form consumes roughly the lower 60–70% of the bordered box height on a typical screen, giving it clear primary visual importance within the page’s main container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the bordered container, expanding vertically as needed; it pushes the bottom of the container down without overlapping or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search autocomplete is positioned near the very top of the page within the same horizontal band as the page title and login strip. The band itself sits flush with the top content margin, with about 16–24px padding from the left and right viewport edges. On this band, the page title 'Contact' is left-aligned, and the search form is aligned to the far right of the same line, appearing as a small inline text input with a simple submit button, matching default browser styling. The search input and button together occupy roughly 25–35% of the page width on large screens, shrinking fluidly on smaller widths but always remaining on a single line with the title and login elements when possible. Vertical space above and below this header band is minimal (around 8–12px), so the search feels tucked into the utility strip rather than floating in isolation. When autocomplete suggestions appear, they drop down directly beneath the search input, left-aligned with the input’s left edge and matching its width, using a plain, lightly bordered list style consistent with the site’s minimalism. The dropdown temporarily overlays the area where the breadcrumb or top of the main content box would be, but does not permanently shift or reflow surrounding elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the top header strip, participating in normal flow for its initial position. Autocomplete results appear as an absolutely positioned dropdown relative to the input, overlaying content below without affecting layout flow.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Page that loads and displays the legal Terms of Use content and allows users to search the site from within the same view.

core_features: Load and render the Terms of Use content via the static page content interface using a page slug.
Provide a dynamic in-page table of contents based on the loaded Terms content to help users navigate sections.
Support direct linking to specific sections of the Terms via URL parameters and in-page anchors.
Offer a site-wide search form that calls the search interface to find HYIP programs and forum threads related to user queries.
Display an optional inline search results preview (programs and forum threads) based on URL parameters without leaving the Terms page.
user_workflows: View Terms of Use: 1) User navigates to terms.html (e.g., via footer link). 2) On page load, the page determines the static content slug (from routing or an optional page_slug URL parameter). 3) The page calls getStaticPageContent with the resolved slug. 4) The Terms of Use title and HTML body returned by the interface are rendered. 5) The user scrolls through the content to read the terms.

Navigate within Terms sections: 1) After getStaticPageContent returns, the page parses headings or anchors in body_html to build a sections list. 2) The table of contents component displays these sections as links. 3) When the user clicks a section link, the URL is updated with a section parameter (or anchor) corresponding to that section. 4) The page scrolls to the target section in the Terms content. 5) If the user loads the page with a section parameter already set, the page scrolls directly to that section after content is rendered.

Search from Terms page: 1) The user types a query into the search input in the header or within the page. 2) The user optionally chooses a search scope (e.g., all content, programs only, or forum threads only) using a control that maps to the type_filter parameter. 3) On search submit, the page updates the URL parameters (e.g., q, type_filter, search_page, search_page_size). 4) The inline search results component detects those parameters and calls searchSite with the corresponding values. 5) The component renders separate result lists for programs and forum threads, using the returned Program and ForumThread data. 6) The user can click any search result to navigate to the relevant program or forum thread detail page.

Paginate inline search results on Terms page: 1) When search results are visible, the user interacts with pagination controls for programs or forum threads. 2) The pagination control updates the relevant URL parameter (e.g., search_page) to the chosen page number while preserving the existing query and type_filter. 3) The inline search results component observes the URL change, calls searchSite again with the updated pagination parameters, and refreshes the visible results. 4) The user remains on terms.html while browsing result pages.
interactions: On initial page load, trigger a call to getStaticPageContent using a slug resolved for this Terms page (either from a fixed route configuration or from a page_slug URL parameter) and bind the returned title and body_html to the Terms content component.

When the user clicks a table of contents entry, update the URL (e.g., by adding or changing a section query parameter or hash fragment) and scroll the Terms content container to the corresponding heading or anchor element.
When the user manually modifies the URL to include a section parameter or anchor, the page, after rendering the Terms content, reads that value and scrolls to the relevant section automatically.

When the user types in the search input and submits the form, read the query and the selected scope, update the URL parameters for q, type_filter, and reset pagination parameters, then invoke searchSite via the inline search results component.

When search results are visible and the user changes page via pagination controls, update the search_page URL parameter and re-trigger searchSite with the new page value, then re-render the Program and ForumThread result lists.

When the user clicks a search result item (program or thread), navigate to the appropriate detail URL with that item's identifier encoded in URL parameters (the destination page, not this Terms page, handles its own data loading).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms of Use page relies on URL parameters to control which static content is fetched, which section of that content is focused, and whether inline search is active:

1) Static content selection:
- If a page_slug query parameter is present (e.g., terms.html?page_slug=terms-of-use), the page calls getStaticPageContent with that page_slug value and binds the returned title and body_html to the Terms content display.
- If page_slug is not present, the page uses the slug associated with this route as configured at application level (outside this page's implementation) and passes that to getStaticPageContent.

2) Section focus within Terms:
- An optional section query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=some-section-id) or URL hash is used to identify a specific section within the Terms body_html.
- After getStaticPageContent completes and the Terms HTML is mounted, the page checks for the section value. If present, it locates the element whose id or named anchor matches that value and scrolls to it.
- When the user clicks an entry in the table of contents, the page updates the section parameter (or hash) accordingly and triggers the same scroll behavior.

3) Search state:
- An optional q parameter represents the search query. If q is present and non-empty, the inline search results component treats search mode as active and calls searchSite.
- An optional type_filter parameter narrows the search scope (for example, to only programs or only forum threads). The exact values used are determined at application level; this page only passes through whatever value is present in the URL.
- Optional search_page and search_page_size parameters control pagination and page size when calling searchSite. If they are not provided, the component uses pagination defaults determined by global configuration, not hardcoded within this page.
- On search form submission, the page sets or updates q and type_filter, and resets search_page to represent the first page of results.
- When pagination controls are used, only search_page (and, if the user changes page size, search_page_size) are updated, while q and type_filter remain unchanged.

4) History and navigation:
- All changes to section, q, type_filter, search_page, and search_page_size are reflected in the browser's URL (e.g., via pushState or by reloading with updated query parameters) so that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same view and search state of the Terms page.
- No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used for this page's state; all user-visible state is either derived from the current URL or from live interface calls.

components.functionality: Fetches and renders the Terms of Use static content, including title and HTML body, based on a slug, and exposes hooks for section-based navigation.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, determine the effective page_slug (prefer the page_slug URL parameter if provided, otherwise use the route-configured slug for this Terms page) and call getStaticPageContent with that slug. Bind the returned title to the page-level heading and body_html to an HTML-rendering container, sanitizing as required by the application.

onContentRendered: After body_html is inserted into the DOM, emit an event or callback that allows other components (such as the table of contents and section scroller) to parse headings and anchor elements.

onSectionParamChange: When the URL's section parameter or hash changes, locate the corresponding element within the rendered body_html and scroll it into view using the application's scrolling behavior.

components.functionality: Builds and displays a dynamic table of contents for the Terms content by inspecting the rendered HTML and provides clickable links to navigate between sections.
event_handlers: onContentAnalyzed: When notified by the terms-static-content component that body_html has been rendered, traverse the DOM subtree containing the Terms content, identify heading tags or dedicated anchor markers, and construct an internal list of sections, each with an id and label derived from the content.

onTocItemClick: When the user clicks an item in the table of contents, update the browser URL to include or change the section query parameter (or hash) to the section's id, and trigger a scroll action via terms-static-content's section navigation handler.

onUrlChange: When the URL changes due to navigation or back/forward actions, detect changes to the section parameter and update the active state of the relevant TOC entry so that the currently focused section is highlighted.

components.functionality: Collects a search query and optional scope from the user and updates the URL to drive the inline search results via the searchSite interface.
event_handlers: onInit: On component mount, read existing URL parameters (q and type_filter). Populate the search input field and scope selector with these values so that the search form reflects the current search state, if any.

onInputChange: As the user types or changes the scope selector, update the component's internal state but do not immediately call searchSite to avoid unnecessary requests; the actual search is triggered on form submission.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission behavior, read the current input and scope values, and update the URL parameters q and type_filter accordingly. Reset the search_page parameter to represent the first page of results. After the URL is updated, the inline search results component will invoke searchSite based on the new parameters.

onClear: If the user clears the query (for example, via a clear button), remove q, type_filter, search_page, and search_page_size from the URL so that the Terms page returns to non-search mode, and inline search results (if any) are hidden.

components.functionality: Displays an inline preview of search results (programs and forum threads) on the Terms page by calling searchSite using query and pagination information encoded in the URL.
event_handlers: onInit: On component mount, inspect the URL for q and type_filter parameters. If q is present and not empty, treat inline search as active and call searchSite with query=q, type_filter from the URL (if provided), and pagination parameters search_page and search_page_size (if present). Bind the returned program_results, forum_thread_results, total_program_results, and total_forum_thread_results to the result lists and summary indicators.

onUrlChange: Watch for changes to q, type_filter, search_page, and search_page_size in the URL (for example, due to user submitting a new search or using pagination controls). Whenever these parameters change, re-call searchSite with the updated values and refresh the rendered results.

onProgramResultClick: When the user clicks a program result, navigate to the program details page using the Program object's identifier in the destination URL. This navigation leaves the Terms page and is handled by the program details page.
onThreadResultClick: When the user clicks a forum thread result, navigate to the forum thread page using the ForumThread object's identifier in the destination URL.

onPaginatePrograms: When the user interacts with the pagination controls related to program_results, update the search_page URL parameter (and optionally search_page_size if the user changes the page size for programs) and re-trigger the onUrlChange behavior, which will call searchSite again with the new pagination values.

onPaginateThreads: When the user interacts with pagination for forum_thread_results (if presented separately), update the same search_page parameter or a threads-specific page parameter as defined by the application routing, then re-trigger searchSite via onUrlChange.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column flow. At the very top-left, a bold text page title ('Terms of Use'). Aligned to the top-right on the same horizontal row, the header search form appears as a compact inline strip (label, input, button). Directly below, spanning the full width, the Terms TOC appears as a thin horizontal line of section links, visually similar to a breadcrumb. Beneath that, a large bordered content box fills most of the page height, containing the terms static content first and, when applicable, inline search results stacked below it inside the same box.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column flow. At the very top-left, a bold text page title ('Terms of Use'). Aligned to the top-right on the same horizontal row, the header search form appears as a compact inline strip (label, input, button). Directly below, spanning the full width, the Terms TOC appears as a thin horizontal line of section links, visually similar to a breadcrumb. Beneath that, a large bordered content box fills most of the page height, containing the terms static content first and, when applicable, inline search results stacked below it inside the same box.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain modest outer margins (about 16–24px) from the browser edges. The header row (title + search) and the TOC occupy minimal vertical space at the top, with tight gaps (8–12px) between them. The main bordered content container occupies the majority of the vertical space and most of the horizontal space (around 80–90% of the page width, centered or left-aligned within the body margin), mirroring the mockup’s dominance of the main list box. Inside this box, terms content consumes the bulk of height; the inline search results expand below as needed, pushing the page height down without changing the structure.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use high information density consistent with an old-school forum: small line-height, compact paragraphs, and minimal padding. Section gaps are small (8–12px). The search form uses tightly spaced inline controls with 2–4px internal padding. The TOC links sit close together in a single line or two lines if wrapped, separated by minimal delimiters (e.g., pipes), mimicking a breadcrumb. The main box uses about 8–12px inner padding and tight spacing between headings, paragraphs, and any result items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The reading flow starts at the upper-left page title, immediately sweeps right to the inline search form, then drops down to the horizontal TOC line. From there, the eye moves directly into the large bordered content area, scanning down through the terms sections. If search results are present, the eye continues straight down within the same box into the results list, reading each result line left-to-right. Overall eye movement is strongly vertical with minimal lateral wandering, preserving the simple, utilitarian forum feel.

layout.overall_description: The page resembles an early-2000s forum utility page: a white background, black body text, blue links, and thin gray borders. At the top-left, the plain text title 'Terms of Use' appears in a slightly larger, bold font. On the same horizontal line, flush to the right side of the content area, sits a compact inline search strip with a label, text field, and a basic rectangular button. A small gap below leads to a single, thin line of section links functioning as a table of contents—simple blue text links separated by small symbols, left-aligned and visually similar to a breadcrumb bar. A slightly larger vertical gap below this TOC introduces a large rectangular bordered box that dominates the rest of the viewport. Inside this box, the terms content is rendered as dense, left-aligned text with small section headings and tight spacing, stretching down the page. When a search is performed, a small, bold 'Search results' label appears near the bottom of this box, followed by a plain list of result entries, each on its own line, matching the dense list style of a forum. Everything scrolls together in a single column, with no decorative elements or modern UI flourish—only utilitarian text and basic HTML controls arranged in a straightforward vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms static content lives inside the main bordered content box, starting at the very top of that box just below its inner padding. The box itself is centered or left-aligned within the page content area and spans roughly 80–90% of the page width, with about 16–24px margin from the browser edges. Within the box, there is about 8–12px of padding on all sides. The terms content is arranged as a vertical sequence of headings and paragraphs: section headings appear left-aligned, slightly bolder than body text but not oversized; paragraphs follow directly beneath each heading with a small vertical gap of about 8px. The line height is tight, only slightly larger than the font size, so that many lines fit on the screen. Any lists (e.g., numbered clauses) are indented minimally from the left but still align visually with the dense, text-first aesthetic. This block of content occupies the upper portion of the bordered box and will typically take up the majority of the box’s vertical height. As the user scrolls, the terms content is the main continuous text flow, and it ends a short distance (around 12–16px) above where the inline search results begin, leaving a small breathing space but no heavy separator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrolling flow; expands vertically as needed with no fixed height. It maintains its position at the top of the bordered container and resizes fluidly with the container width, reflowing text as the viewport width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms TOC is positioned directly below the top header row (title + search form), acting visually like a breadcrumb bar. It spans the full width of the content area (matching the eventual width of the main bordered box) but is not itself boxed; it sits directly on the white background. The TOC is left-aligned, starting flush with the same left margin as the page title. It is composed of multiple section links presented inline on a single horizontal line where possible, separated by simple delimiters such as a greater-than symbol or vertical bar. Each link is plain blue text with standard underline or default browser styling, and the overall text is slightly smaller or equal in size to the body text, emphasizing utility over importance. Vertical spacing is tight: there is a small gap (about 8px) between the header row and the TOC, and another similar gap between the TOC and the top of the main bordered content box. If the viewport is narrow and the links wrap, they simply continue on the next line, keeping the TOC as a thin multi-line block with no special layout adjustment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the main document flow. It does not stick to the top of the viewport; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page. Its width follows the overall page content width and wraps naturally when constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search form is placed in the same top row as the 'Terms of Use' title, aligned to the right edge of the main content area. The title sits on the left; the search form sits on the right, both sharing the same vertical baseline. The form is a compact, single-line set of controls: a short text label (e.g., 'Search'), a single-line text input field, and a rectangular submit button. These elements are arranged horizontally with minimal gaps (about 4–8px) between them. The text input width is modest—roughly one-third of the total header width on a typical desktop screen—so the entire form occupies about 30–40% of the horizontal space. Padding inside the input and button is minimal (around 2–4px), in line with early browser default controls. There is no additional framing or background; the form sits directly on the white page background. On narrower viewports, when there isn’t enough room for the title and search to share the same line, the search form naturally wraps to the line just below the title while still remaining above the TOC, preserving a tight stacked arrangement with small vertical gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page within the normal document flow. It aligns to the right of the content column and reflows responsively when horizontal space is constrained, but it does not float or stick; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline search results are positioned inside the same large bordered box that contains the terms static content, always appearing beneath the terms text. There is a small vertical gap (around 12–16px) between the final paragraph of the terms and the start of the search results section. At the top of the results area, a simple label such as 'Search results:' appears in bold or slightly emphasized text, left-aligned and in the same width as the terms content. Directly beneath the label, the results are listed in a dense vertical sequence, similar in feeling to a forum’s post list: each result occupies a single line or a tight two-line block, with the main result title as a blue link followed by a short descriptor or snippet in regular text. The lines are spaced closely (4–8px between lines), maximizing the number of visible results within the viewport. No additional border surrounds the results; they rely on the encompassing main box for framing. If there are many results, this section extends the total height of the bordered container and page, keeping a uniform left alignment with the rest of the body text. If there are no results, this entire block is absent, and the terms box ends shortly after the terms content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main bordered container and appears only when search actions produce results. It grows vertically as needed, pushing the page’s scrollable height downward but never changing its position relative to the terms content above.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically managed privacy policy content and allows users to search the site from this page.

core_features: Load and render the privacy policy content via getStaticPageContent based on a page_slug derived from the URL.
Support deep-linking into specific privacy policy sections via URL parameters so users can jump directly to relevant topics (e.g., data collection, notifications, account settings).
Provide an on-page search form that uses searchSite to search across HYIP programs and forum threads without leaving the privacy policy context.
Display and paginate search results (programs and forum threads) returned by searchSite when a query is present in the URL.
user_workflows:
View the privacy policy:
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the footer or opens privacy.html directly.
2. The page router sets or derives a page_slug in the URL query string that identifies the privacy policy static content.
3. The privacy-page-loader component reads the page_slug from the URL and calls getStaticPageContent with that value.
4. The response (title and body_html) is passed to the privacy-content-viewer, which renders the privacy policy text.
5. If a section URL parameter is present, the viewer scrolls to or highlights the corresponding section within body_html.

Navigate to a specific section of the policy (e.g., from an external link or internal table of contents):
1. A link to the privacy policy includes a section query parameter in the URL (for example, privacy.html?section=<section-identifier>), or the user clicks a table-of-contents entry inside the page.
2. The privacy-toc or privacy-content-viewer updates the section parameter in the URL using the history API (without reloading the page).
3. The privacy-content-viewer listens for changes to the section parameter and scrolls to the matching section anchor or heading inside body_html.
4. The user can use the browser's back/forward buttons to move between previously selected sections because each state change is reflected in the URL.

Search the site from the privacy policy page:
1. The user types a query into the privacy-search-form input.
2. On submit, the form updates the URL with query-related parameters (q for the search term, optional type_filter for narrowing to programs or forum threads, and page for pagination), then triggers a search.
3. The privacy-search-results component reads q, type_filter, and page from the URL and calls searchSite with these parameters.
4. searchSite returns program_results, forum_thread_results, and total counts; privacy-search-results renders the results below the policy or in a dedicated results area on the page.
5. The user changes the query or type filter using the form; the URL parameters are updated and searchSite is called again.
6. When the user changes pages (e.g., clicks 'Next' or a specific page number), the privacy-search-results updates the page parameter in the URL and re-calls searchSite.

Switch between reading the policy and inspecting search results:
1. The user arrives with no q parameter; the page shows only the static privacy policy content and table of contents.
2. When the user submits a search (resulting in a non-empty q parameter), the privacy-search-results component becomes active and renders search results based on searchSite output.
3. The user can clear the query via the search form; the form removes q (and any related search parameters) from the URL.
4. Once q is removed, the privacy-search-results component hides its output and the page returns to showing only the policy content (while keeping the user’s scroll position if feasible).
interactions:
Clicking the footer 'Privacy Policy' link:
- Navigates to privacy.html with an appropriate page_slug and optional section parameter in the URL.
- Triggers initial content loading via getStaticPageContent.

Page load / URL change:
- On initial load or any URL change that affects page_slug, the privacy-page-loader fires an onInit or onPageSlugChange handler.
- Calls getStaticPageContent with the current page_slug value.
- On URL changes that affect section, the privacy-content-viewer scrolls to the corresponding section.

Clicking a table-of-contents entry:
- The privacy-toc component intercepts the click.
- Updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., via history.pushState) to match the selected section identifier.
- Triggers the privacy-content-viewer onSectionChange handler to scroll to or highlight that section of the policy.

Typing into the on-page search field:
- The privacy-search-form onChange handler updates its internal input state but does not query the API yet.
- When the user presses Enter or clicks the search button, the onSubmit handler updates the URL with q, type_filter, and resets page to the first page.
- Triggers privacy-search-results to call searchSite.

Using search filters (type_filter):
- When the user selects a filter (e.g., programs only or forum threads only) in the privacy-search-form, the onFilterChange handler updates the type_filter parameter in the URL and resets page.
- privacy-search-results observes the URL change and calls searchSite with the updated type_filter.

Paginating through search results:
- In privacy-search-results, clicking a pagination control triggers an onPageChange handler.
- The handler updates the page parameter in the URL.
- The component re-calls searchSite with the same q and type_filter but the new page value.

Clicking on a search result:
- For a program result, clicking the title navigates the user to the program detail page using a URL pattern that includes the program identifier as URL parameters.
- For a forum thread result, clicking the title navigates the user to the forum thread page using a URL pattern that includes the thread identifier as URL parameters.
- These navigations do not modify any state on the privacy policy page itself but allow the user to return using the browser back button, preserving the previous search query parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters. Example parameters and their effects:
- page_slug: Identifies which static content to load via getStaticPageContent. The privacy-page-loader reads this parameter and calls getStaticPageContent({ page_slug: <value-from-URL> }). This parameter is required for content loading and can be altered only by navigation or routing, not hardcoded in the page logic.
- section: Indicates which part of the privacy policy the user is focused on (e.g., data collection, cookies, notifications). When section changes, the privacy-content-viewer onSectionChange handler scrolls to the corresponding anchor or heading within body_html. Links within the table of contents and external links can set section, making the page deep-linkable.
- q: The search query string. When q is present and non-empty, the privacy-search-results component becomes active and calls searchSite({ query: q, type_filter: <from URL or default>, page: <from URL or default>, page_size: <from configuration> }). When q is absent, privacy-search-results does not call searchSite and remains hidden.
- type_filter: Optional filter to limit searchSite to specific resource types (e.g., programs, forum threads, or both). The privacy-search-form component controls this parameter. When changed, it updates type_filter in the URL, resets page, and triggers a new search.
- page: The current search results page number for pagination. The privacy-search-results component reads page from the URL and passes it to searchSite. When the user changes pages, page is updated in the URL so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the current results page.
URL parameters are the single source of truth for state. Components subscribe to URL changes (or router state changes based on the URL) and re-run getStaticPageContent or searchSite as necessary. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used for this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the privacy policy page by reading the page_slug from the URL and fetching the corresponding static content via getStaticPageContent. Passes the resulting title and body_html to child components for rendering and anchors/table-of-contents generation.

Renders the privacy policy title and body_html returned by getStaticPageContent, supports internal anchors, and responds to the section URL parameter to scroll to or highlight specific sections of the policy.

Builds a table of contents by parsing headings or markers from the body_html provided by getStaticPageContent. Provides clickable entries that update the section URL parameter, enabling deep linking and quick navigation within the policy.

Provides an input field and optional type filter controls for searching site content directly from the privacy policy page. On submit, synchronizes the query and filter with URL parameters and triggers searchSite via the privacy-search-results component.

Reads q, type_filter, and page from the URL. When q is present, calls searchSite with these parameters and renders program_results and forum_thread_results, including pagination controls that update the page URL parameter. Hides itself when q is not present in the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column fluid layout, likely based on simple block elements or a basic HTML table for some parts rather than a formal grid system.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, full-width, top‑to‑bottom column. Outside these components, the page still follows the mockup pattern: page title at the top-left (“Privacy Policy”), a narrow login strip aligned to the right, then a breadcrumb line below. Directly beneath the breadcrumb, the components appear stacked: first the privacy-page-loader (only when active), then one large bordered content box containing the privacy-toc and privacy-content-viewer with the privacy-search-form embedded at the top of that box. A second bordered box for privacy-search-results sits below the first, following the same simple, linear stacking pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, full-width, top‑to‑bottom column. Outside these components, the page still follows the mockup pattern: page title at the top-left (“Privacy Policy”), a narrow login strip aligned to the right, then a breadcrumb line below. Directly beneath the breadcrumb, the components appear stacked: first the privacy-page-loader (only when active), then one large bordered content box containing the privacy-toc and privacy-content-viewer with the privacy-search-form embedded at the top of that box. A second bordered box for privacy-search-results sits below the first, following the same simple, linear stacking pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content area is slightly inset from the browser edges, spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width and centered, leaving simple white margins of about 16–24px on the left and right. Vertically, gaps between the header, login strip, breadcrumb, loader, and each bordered box are small (around 8–12px), keeping everything compact. The primary privacy policy box (with TOC + content) consumes most of the vertical space by default. The search area (TOC and search form row) at the top of that box occupies only a thin band, while the bulk of the height is taken by the text in the privacy-content-viewer. When present, the search-results box extends the page downward and may also grow tall, but it remains clearly subordinate to the main policy content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain high information density throughout. Paragraphs of the privacy policy have tight line-height (around 1.2) and small spacing between paragraphs. The TOC entries are listed vertically with almost no extra padding beyond basic line spacing. The search form uses compact, inline controls with minimal internal padding (2–4px) and barely any extra margin between the label, input field, and button. Search result items are stacked closely with only a small vertical gap (4–8px) between lines, mimicking a utilitarian forum or early-2000s list style.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The reading flow is strongly vertical and left-aligned. Eyes go from the bold “Privacy Policy” page title (outside these components) straight down to the top edge of the main bordered box. Inside that box, the eye first catches the TOC at the top-left, then scans rightward across the same horizontal band to the search form on the top-right. From there, the eye drops down into the main privacy-content-viewer text and continues scrolling downward through sections. If a search is performed, attention returns briefly to the search form and then continues straight down into the search-results box below, scanning its list items top-to-bottom in a linear fashion without any side columns or zig-zag patterns.

layout.overall_description: The page feels like a classic, minimalist forum page: plain white background, default browser fonts, and simple bordered boxes stacked in a single column. At the very top-left sits the bold text “Privacy Policy,” with a narrow login strip to its right and a breadcrumb line underneath, all tightly spaced. Directly below the breadcrumb, a small status line for loading appears only when needed. Beneath that, a large, light-bordered rectangle spans most of the page width, sitting in the center with modest left and right margins. The top band of this box is a compact control strip: on the left, a simple text-based table of contents lists the main sections of the privacy policy line by line; on the right, a narrow, inline search form uses a label, text input, and submit button in one horizontal row. Below this strip, the bulk of the box is filled with dense paragraphs of the privacy policy, heading after heading stacked with minimal gaps, giving the feel of a long forum post or terms-of-service document. If the user issues a search, a second box—styled almost identically to the first—appears immediately underneath: its interior holds a plain ordered or bulleted list of search results, each on its own line with link text and small metadata, tightly packed to expose as many results as possible in one screen. Everything flows straight down the page, with no sidebars or decorative elements, creating a strict, utilitarian reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader is positioned directly below the breadcrumb line and immediately above the top edge of the main privacy policy bordered box, aligned with the left edge of the central content column. It appears as a single, plain text line such as “Loading privacy policy…” using the default font and size, with no special styling beyond perhaps italic or lighter weight. Horizontally, it spans the same width as the main content column (about 80–90% of the viewport, centered) but visually occupies only a very thin strip—roughly the height of one line of text plus a tiny vertical padding (4px above and below). When not active, this line disappears completely, allowing the main bordered box to move up so that the breadcrumb sits immediately above it, maintaining the compact stacked layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element in the normal document flow that only occupies vertical space while loading is in progress; when inactive it collapses to zero height, with no overlay or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-viewer sits inside the primary bordered box, directly below the narrow top band that contains the TOC and search form. The box itself is centered on the page, about 80–90% of the viewport width, with a simple 1px border and internal padding of roughly 8–12px on all sides. Within this box, after a small gap (around 8px) under the TOC/search band, the viewer area begins and stretches from the left padding edge to the right padding edge, forming a single, continuous column of text. The content is rendered as basic HTML text: section headings in bold or slightly larger font, followed by dense paragraphs of policy copy with minimal spacing between paragraphs (about 4–8px). There are no side columns, no indents beyond the default, and no extra background color; it resembles a long, serious forum post or article, filling most of the vertical space of this primary box. As the policy grows, the page simply extends downward, and the viewer content remains tightly stacked, with the bottom of this text column sitting just above the bottom padding of the box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width content area within its bordered container, expanding vertically as needed; scrollable only via the page scroll, not as an independent scrollable region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-toc occupies the top-left portion of the primary bordered box, forming the left side of the thin control band at the top of that box. Inside the box’s 8–12px padding, the TOC begins at the top-left corner and extends downward for a short distance, typically listing 4–10 section links. Each TOC entry is a simple left-aligned text link on its own line, with minimal vertical spacing (about 4px) between items, creating a dense vertical stack. Horizontally, the TOC occupies roughly the left 60–65% of the box’s internal width, leaving the remaining right 35–40% for the inline search form. The TOC does not introduce any extra visual framing beyond the box border itself—no shading or sub-boxes—just plain text links, giving the impression of a classic, text-only contents list. The bottom of the TOC aligns roughly with, or slightly above, the bottom of the search form on the right, together forming a compact top strip above the main body text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top-left inside the primary bordered box, participating in the normal flow; it does not stick or float while scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-search-form is positioned inside the same primary bordered box as the privacy-toc, aligned to the top-right within the box’s padding. It shares the top horizontal band with the TOC, effectively forming a single thin control strip: while the TOC occupies the left 60–65% of the width, the search form fills the remaining right 35–40%. The form’s elements are laid out in a single inline row: a short label such as “Search:” on the far left of this right-hand segment, then a medium-width text input, followed by a compact submit button. All controls use default browser styling—rectangular inputs with minimal internal padding (2–4px) and no custom borders or icons. The entire search form row has very little vertical height, roughly the height of the input plus 2–4px of padding above and below, matching the compact aesthetic of the login strip in the original mockup. There is a small horizontal gap (about 4–8px) between the TOC and the search form, but no dividing line; they simply sit side by side within the same top band. The space immediately below this band transitions directly into the privacy-content-viewer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned inline form within the top band of the primary bordered box; it does not float or stick, and it moves with the rest of the content as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-search-results component is placed in its own bordered box that appears directly below the primary privacy policy box, maintaining the same centered, single-column alignment and width (about 80–90% of the viewport). There is a small vertical gap (around 8–12px) between the bottom of the primary box and the top of this search-results box, mirroring the tight stacking used elsewhere. The box has the same simple 1px border and 8–12px internal padding, so it visually matches the main content box. Inside, the results are laid out as a dense vertical list, reminiscent of an old forum or directory listing: each result is on its own line, starting at the left, possibly with an ordinal number followed by a blue text link for the result title, then a hyphen and brief metadata or snippet in normal or slightly lighter text. Lines are tightly spaced, with only a small vertical gap of 4–8px between items, so many results can be viewed in a single screen. The list spans the full internal width of the box, and the box expands vertically as more results are present. If there are no results, the box may either be omitted entirely or reduced to a single compact line of explanatory text like “No results found,” still aligned to the left inside the same bordered frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level container stacked directly under the primary bordered box; it appears only when search results are available or when needed, expanding vertically with the number of results and scrolling with the page.
